claims.claim: A self-heating rate of ≥2 °C min-1 was defined as the initiation of thermal runaway in this study, as no specifications on thermal runaway of electrochemical devices were found in literature.
claims.quote: As there are no specifications on thermal runaway of electrochemical devices found in literature, a self-heating rate of ≥2 °C min-1 had been chosen in this work to define the beginning of thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The ICR18650HE4 cell aged by storage at 60 °C did not transgress into thermal runaway, whereas the same cell type in pristine condition and aged by cyclisation did undergo thermal runaway.
claims.quote: After aging, the low energy cell ICR18650HE4 did not transgress into thermal runaway. ... The low capacity ICR18650HE4 cells exhibited the least reactivity after aging by storage, making them somewhat safer under these conditions.

claims.claim: Aging by storage at 60 °C changed the first-venting gas composition from predominantly CO2 to predominantly C2H2 for all tested cell types.
claims.quote: Aging by storage drastically changed the vent gas composition at this stage, as most of the detected gas was C2H2 for all the cells.

claims.claim: The total heat released during thermal runaway for NCR18650BF aged by storage at 60 °C was 81% of that of the pristine cell, and for INR18650-35E aged by storage it was 61% of the pristine cell value.
claims.quote: Cells aged this way showed the lowest emission of heat in comparison to new cells – 81 % for NCR18650BF, 61 % for INR18650-35E and no thermal runaway in case of ICR18650HE4.

claims.claim: The main component of vent gas during thermal runaway was CO, making the vent gas a flammable and toxic mixture, and this was roughly similar for all tested cells regardless of aging condition.
claims.quote: The main component of the vent gas during thermal runaway was CO, making the vent gas a flammable and toxic mixture. It poses a high risk of fire and explosion in regard to the high temperature associated with the event of thermal runaway.

claims.claim: Cells that had reached the automotive drop-out criterion of 80% remaining capacity still posed a hazard in case of thermally induced failure.
claims.quote: Even cells which have reached the drop-out criterion of 80 % remaining capacity are still potentially hazardous.

claims.claim: ICR18650HE4 aged by cyclisation released only 6% of the gas volume compared to the pristine specimen during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: after aging by cyclisation it released 6 % of the volume compared to the pristine specimen.

claims.claim: CO2 is attributed mainly to decarboxylation of the organic carbonate electrolyte, CO is attributed to oxidation of carbonaceous species by oxygen released from the oxide electrode, and H2 emission appears to be a product of the reduction of H2O from combustion of carbohydrates.
claims.quote: CO2 is mainly deriving from decarboxylation of the organic carbonate electrolyte; CO is attributed to oxidation of carbonaceous species by oxygen released from the oxide electrode. The H2 emission appears to be a product of the reduction of H2O deriving from the combustion of carbohydrates.

claims.claim: For squeezing pressures at or below 4000 N applied to 100% SOC pouch LIBs during flat heating, the TR triggering temperature is not significantly shifted compared to the non-squeezed state and is stable at approximately 220°C, which is 20–42°C higher than flat heating alone.
claims.quote: when the squeezing pressure does not exceed 4000 N, the TR triggering temperature is not significantly shifted (stable at approximately 220 C), and higher (20～42 C) than the flat heating alone.

claims.claim: After the squeezing pressure reaches 5000 N on 100% SOC pouch LIBs, the TR triggering temperature drops to approximately 100°C and slowly decreases as the squeezing pressure further increases, with the dominant TR trigger mechanism shifting from heating to mechanical extrusion-induced internal short circuit.
claims.quote: After the extrusion force reaches 5000 N, the TR triggering temperature drops to ~100 C, and slowly decreases as the squeezing pressure further increases. This is because the thickness of the battery decreases more with increasing temperature under high-intensity mechanical extrusion, and the internal deformation reaches the level of triggering an internal short circuit at a lower temperature.

claims.claim: In the squeezed state, the battery TR-related smoke production window narrows as squeezing pressure increases, reducing the time available to detect thermal runaway by monitoring gas composition.
claims.quote: in the squeezed condition, the battery TR-related smoke production window narrows as the squeezing pressure increases. The time interval between the smoke ejection and TR flame generation decreases, and the amount of produced smoke decreases as well... Therefore, it will be more difficult and less timely to predict battery thermal runaway by detecting the gas composition in the battery pack.

claims.claim: For squeezing pressures above 5000 N on 100% SOC pouch LIBs, voltage drop occurs concurrently with TR onset and pre-TR voltage fluctuations disappear, making voltage monitoring ineffective as a TR predictor.
claims.quote: For squeezing pressures above 5000 N, the TR triggering temperature decreased to approximately 100 C, the voltage dropped concurrently with the TR onset, the voltage fluctuation before the TR disappeared, and TR triggering was mostly owing to the extrusion-induced mechanical damage. The significant decrease in the TR starting temperature and the disappearance of voltage fluctuations for squeezing pressures above 5000 N indicates that the monitoring of the battery TR in terms of its temperature and voltage becomes irrelevant.

claims.claim: The maximal surface temperature of squeezed 100% SOC pouch LIBs after TR peaks at a squeezing pressure of 5000 N (reaching approximately 600°C compared to 440°C for non-squeezed), and then decreases slowly as squeezing pressure increases further due to increased energy release through intensified injection.
claims.quote: the maximal surface temperature of a squeezed LIB after TR increases from 440 C to ~600 C. The maximal surface temperature of the battery peaks for the squeezing pressure of 5000 N, and then decreases slowly as the squeezing pressure increases. The temperature reaches its highest at 5000 N because the TR trigger of the battery starts to change from heating to mechanical extrusion... After the pressure continues to rise, the battery surface temperature starts to drop due to the increased energy release to the external environment from the increased injection intensity.

claims.claim: The speed of high-temperature ejecta and jet fire during TR of squeezed 100% SOC pouch LIBs can reach 24 m/s.
claims.quote: the speed of high-temperature ejecta and jet fire can reach 24 m/s (calculated from V=S/T based on the length of the molten mass flight path and exposure time per frame in Supplementary Movie 1).

claims.claim: For squeezing pressures above 5000 N, the jelly roll structure of the pouch LIB after TR onset gradually disappears and all remnants are pressed together into a hard slab, whereas for forces under 5000 N the battery wreckage exhibits localized loosening with ejection channels.
claims.quote: For forces under 5000 N, the battery wreckage exhibits localized loosening, and this loosening part is the pathway for ejecting internal battery materials during the TR. For squeezing pressures above 5000 N, the degree of the battery wreckage fragmentation increases... the jelly roll structure of the cell after the TR onset gradually disappears as the squeezing pressure increases, and all of the remnants are pressed together into a hard slab under high pressure and high temperature.

claims.claim: Under a heating power of 500 W/m², the onset temperature of thermal runaway for the LTO anode cell is 437 K, which is higher than the 407 K onset temperature for the graphite anode cell.
claims.quote: when the heating power is 500 W m−2, the onset temperature of TR for LTO cell is 437 K, which is higher than that for graphite cell (407 K)

claims.claim: Under a heating power of 500 W/m², the onset time of thermal runaway for the graphite anode cell is 650 s earlier than for the LTO anode cell.
claims.quote: the onset time of TR for the cell with graphite anode is 650 s earlier than that of the cells with LTO anode

claims.claim: The peak temperature of thermal runaway for the LTO anode cell is 705 K, which is much lower than the 917 K peak temperature for the graphite anode cell, due to the higher enthalpy released by the reaction between graphite anode and electrolyte compared to lithiated LTO.
claims.quote: the peak temperature of TR for LTO cell is 705 K, which is much lower than that of 917 K for the cell with graphite anode... it is because the reaction between graphite anode and electrolyte releases more enthalpy due to the higher energy density of lithiated graphite compared to the lithiated LTO anode

claims.claim: LTO anode cells lack SEI layer protection because the lithiation potential of LTO limits reduction of electrolyte components, which causes slower temperature rise at the SEI decomposition stage and consequently longer onset time to thermal runaway compared to graphite anode cells.
claims.quote: there is no SEI layer protection on the surface of LTO, since its lithiation potential limits reduction of electrolyte components... the exothermic process due to the decomposition of SEI layer to accumulated heat lacks for LTO cells, which leads that the temperature rise of the LTO cell is slower than the graphite cell at the point of SEI layer decomposition

claims.claim: Under a heating power of 500 W/m², the thermal runaway onset temperatures for different cathode materials are 407 K (LCO), 437 K (NCA), 457 K (NCM), and 475 K (LFP), establishing a thermal stability order of LFP > NCM > NCA > LCO.
claims.quote: the TR onset temperatures are 407 K, 437 K, 457 K and 475 K for LCO cell, NCA cell, NCM cell and LFP cell, respectively

claims.claim: The peak thermal runaway temperatures for different cathode materials are approximately 780 K (LCO), 929 K (NCA), 815 K (NCM), and 717 K (LFP), establishing a thermal safety order of LFP > LCO > NCM > NCA.
claims.quote: the peak temperatures are about 780 K, 929 K, 815 K and 717 K for LCO cell, NCA cell, NCM cell and LFP cell respectively. The predictions indicate that the decomposition of NCA cathode releases the most enthalpy compared to others

claims.claim: The thermal safety and thermal stability rankings of cathode materials are consistent between oven tests and local heating conditions, indicating that the type of thermal abuse method does not change the relative ranking.
claims.quote: the thermal safety and thermal stability of the above cathode materials are consistent with previous results measured under oven tests, which means the change of thermal abuse measures does not impact the thermal safety and thermal stability ranks of LIBs, regardless of oven tests or local heating

claims.claim: Increasing the separator melting point increases the thermal runaway onset temperature and onset time, but does not affect the peak temperature of thermal runaway once it occurs.
claims.quote: the onset temperature increases with the increasing melting point of separator... the peak temperature keeps the same for the cell with various separators, which indicates the different melting points of separator materials have little impact on the release of electrical and chemical energy stored inside the cell once TR occurs

claims.claim: When the separator melting point increases from 408 K (PE) to 438 K (PP), the thermal runaway onset time is delayed by 215 s, but when the melting point increases further from 438 K to 473 K (ceramic), the onset time is only delayed by 53 s, indicating diminishing returns at higher melting points.
claims.quote: as the melting point of cell separator increases from 408 K to 438 K (from PE to PP), the time of initiating TR reduces by 215 s. However, as the melting point increases from 438 K to 473 K, the onset time is only reduced by 53 s... the cell separator with high melting point can relieve the occurrence of TR to some extent. However, when the melting point of separator is up to a certain value, the effects of relief may be no longer remarkable

claims.claim: For a ceramic-coated separator (melting point 473 K), the SEI layer and anode volume fractions reduce by 12.33% and 30.07% respectively before thermal runaway, compared to only 4.64% and 0.76% for a PE separator (melting point 408 K), indicating that at higher separator melting points, side-reaction heat contributes significantly to triggering thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the volume fraction of SEI layer and anode reduces about 4.64% and 0.76% before TR for PE separator, which indicates the heat triggering TR mainly comes from external heating. However, the volume fraction of SEI layer and anode reduces about 12.33% and 30.07% before TR for the ceramic coated separator, which indicates the decomposition of SEI layer and anode also provides part of the heat for the triggering TR

claims.claim: Heating near the bottom of the cell triggers thermal runaway earlier than heating at the end or middle positions, with onset times of 200 s, 272 s, and 536 s respectively at 6000 W/m², because bottom heating results in lower average surface temperature and thus lower heat loss.
claims.quote: the heating position near the bottom of cell triggers the TR of LIB earlier than the other two positions... heating on the bottom of the cell shows lower average surface temperature than heating on the end and middle, which makes the heat loss lower than other heating positions in heating stage. In this case, the temperature rise rate is largest, causing TR to occur sooner than other heating positions

claims.claim: The critical total heating power threshold for triggering thermal runaway in a cylindrical 18650 cell lies between 0.75 W and 1.0 W; heating below 0.75 W does not induce thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the total power (heating area S × heating power q) applied to the cells of TR is larger than 1.0 W, and the minimum of total power that does not trigger TR is 0.75 W, which means the critical value of the total power that can trigger TR may be between 0.75 W and 1.0 W. In other words, when the total power applied to the cell surface is less than 0.75 W, such heating does not lead the cell into TR

claims.claim: Under local heating conditions, peak thermal runaway temperature is little influenced by variations in heating power or heating area, in contrast to oven tests where peak temperature increases with oven temperature.
claims.quote: the peak temperature is little influenced by the various heating conditions in this study while the peak temperature increases with the oven temperature under oven test. The reason may lie in that only the side-reactions whose onset temperature is lower than the oven temperature can proceed for the cell under oven test

claims.claim: At an ambient temperature above 300 K with a heating power of 1000 W/m² and heating size of 15 cm², thermal runaway occurs in cells cooled with air velocities less than 5 m/s, while only the cell cooled at 5 m/s can remain in steady-state condition at 320 K ambient temperature.
claims.quote: When the ambient temperature increases to 320 K, only the cell cooled with the air velocity of 5 m s−1 can stay in steady-state condition... When the ambient temperature is larger than 300 K under the heating power of 1000 W m−2 and heating size of 15 cm2, thermal runaway can occur if the air velocity is less than 5 m s−1

claims.claim: The variety of electrolyte material has limited effects on thermal safety and stability compared to other battery materials such as anode and cathode, with the thermal safety order being LiPF6/EC:DEC > LiPF6/PC:DEC > LiPF6/EC:DMC > LiPF6/PC:DMC.
claims.quote: compared to the study of previous sections, it can be found that the change of electrolyte has little significant effect on the peak temperature and onset time of TR, which means it does not play a dominant role in determining thermal behavior of cells under local heating

claims.claim: For 18650 LIBs, as SOC increased, the safety valve open temperature decreased; specifically, the safety valve open temperature was 200.5°C at 0% SOC and 183.1°C at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: as SOC increased, the safety valve open temperature decreased. For instance, the safety valve open temperature of LIBs at 0% and 100% SOCs were 200.5°C and 183.1°C, respectively.

claims.claim: The TR peak temperature of 18650 LIBs increased with increasing SOC; the peak temperature at 100% SOC was 703.6°C, which was more than twice that of the LIB at 0% SOC.
claims.quote: The TR peak temperatures increased with the increasing SOCs. It was proved that the heat released from the LIBs during TR was almost equal to the electrical energy. The peak temperature of the LIB at 100% SOC was 703.6°C, which was more than twice that of the LIB at 0% SOC.

claims.claim: The mass loss of 18650 LIBs at 100% SOC during TR was 15.6 g, which was 2.3 times higher than that of LIBs at 70% SOC (6.7 g), due primarily to the injection of molten aluminum particles.
claims.quote: the mass loss of LIBs at 100% SOC was 15.6 g, 2.3 times higher than that of LIBs at 70% SOC. The more dramatic TR process indicated more energy released. Especially, the injecting of molten aluminum particles caused the most mass loss.

claims.claim: LIB arrays at 70% SOC had the strongest TR propagation capability, with all four LIBs undergoing TR, while LIBs at 30% and 100% SOC did not trigger TR propagation in adjacent cells.
claims.quote: It was found that NO.1 LIBs at 30% and 100% SOCs did not trigger TR of other LIBs. The third LIB of LIB arrays at 50% SOC underwent TR. LIB arrays at 70% SOC had the strongest propagation capability and all the LIBs went into TR.

claims.claim: Although the NO.1 LIB at 100% SOC reached a peak temperature of 794.0°C, the maximum storage heat was only 16.60 kJ, which was less than that of LIBs at 50% SOC (19.26 kJ), and the critical storage heat threshold to trigger TR propagation was determined to be between 16.60 and 19.26 kJ.
claims.quote: Though NO.1 LIB at 100% SOC had the highest Tmax, the storage heat was 16.60 kJ, which was less than that of the LIBs at 50% and 70% SOCs... the value of critical storage heat to trigger TR should be between 16.60 and 19.26 kJ.

claims.claim: Water spray successfully inhibited TR propagation in 18650 LIB arrays, limiting TR to only the directly heated NO.1 LIB, with NO.2 LIB reaching a maximum temperature of only 226.3°C.
claims.quote: Only NO.1 LIB heated by the heater went into TR and NO.2 LIB was heated to a maximum temperature of 226.3°C.

claims.claim: BC ultrafine dry powder and Novec 1230 both failed to inhibit TR propagation in 18650 LIB arrays, with average maximum temperatures of 778.1°C and 783.9°C respectively, showing few differences from the no-agent case (760.4°C).
claims.quote: The average maximum temperatures of LIB arrays under no agents, BC ultraﬁne dry powder, and Novec 1230 were 760.4°C, 778.1°C, and 783.9°C, respectively.

claims.claim: The cooling efficiency ranking of fire extinguishing agents depended on the LIB surface temperature at the time of application: at ~550°C (NO.1 LIB), the rank was ABC dry powder > BC dry powder > water spray > Novec 1230, whereas at ~200°C (NO.2 LIB), the rank was water spray > ABC dry powder > BC dry powder > Novec 1230.
claims.quote: For NO.1 LIB, the ﬁre extinguishing agent cooling efﬁciency rank was ABC ultraﬁne dry powder > BC ultraﬁne dry powder > water spray > Novec 1230. However, for NO.2 LIB, water spray had the best cooling efﬁciency.

claims.claim: Novec 1230 had the poorest cooling effect on LIB surfaces among the four agents tested because its low boiling point of 49.2°C caused it to evaporate before contacting the LIB surfaces when sprayed as small droplets.
claims.quote: The cooling effects of Novec 1230 were limited by releasing method. Spray caused most agents evaporating before contacting with LIB due to the small droplets and low evaporation temperature.

claims.claim: Novec 1230 had the best effect on reducing CO concentration among the four agents, attributed to chain chemical reactions between vaporized Novec 1230 and LIB electrolyte, with maximum CO concentration of 1952 ppm compared to 3897 ppm for BC powder.
claims.quote: Novec 1230 had the better efﬁciency. Novec 1230 had chain chemical reactions with the LIB electrolyte (Wang et al., 2019a), so its effect on decreasing the CO content was much better than other agents... the maximum CO concentration with BC powder and Novec 1230 applying were 3897 ppm and 1952 ppm.

claims.claim: Extensive use of water as a fire suppressant in LIB modules can cause short circuits and potentially greater disasters.
claims.quote: Moreover, the extensive use of water could cause short circuit of LIB modules and cause greater disasters.

claims.claim: The 18650 LIB self-heating onset temperature (Tonset) is 89–130°C, and TR propagation to an adjacent LIB requires that the adjacent cell temperature exceed Tonset by a sufficient margin to overcome heat dissipation losses.
claims.quote: the 18,650 LIB would self-heat when it reached 89–130°C (Tonset)... Since NO.2 LIB was in a nonadiabatic state, TNO:2 needed to be much larger than Tonset to cause TR.

claims.claim: The initial decomposition temperature of the SEI membrane in lithium ion batteries is approximately 80 °C, as observed through accelerating rate calorimeter (ARC) experiments.
claims.quote: Richard et al. observed that the initial decomposition temperature of the SEI membrane was about 80 °C through accelerating rate calorimeter (ARC) experiment.

claims.claim: The polyethylene (PP) separator begins to melt when the battery temperature reaches 130–140 °C, leading to short circuit risks between electrodes.
claims.quote: The polyethylene (PP) separator begins to melt when the temperature further reaches 130−140 °C, leading to short circuits risks between electrodes.

claims.claim: Lithium cobalt oxide (LCO) cathode material decomposes at around 150 °C, releasing oxygen.
claims.quote: Lithium cobalt oxide (LCO) is the earliest cathode material for LIBs, with a decomposition temperature of around 150 °C.

claims.claim: For commercial 1 M LiPF6-EC-DEC-DMC organic electrolyte, the solvent components can be oxidized at over 200 °C by oxygen released from cathode decomposition.
claims.quote: For commercial 1 M LiPF6-EC-DEC-DMC organic electrolyte, the solvent components can be oxidized at over 200 °C by the oxygen released from cathode decomposition.

claims.claim: During high-rate charge–discharge processes, the heat generation rate in LIBs is significantly larger than the heat dissipation rate, resulting in heat accumulation that can increase internal temperature to over 300 °C.
claims.quote: during the high rate charge−discharge process of LIBs, especially in those batteries equipped in electronic devices, the heat generation rate is signiﬁcantly larger than the heat dissipation rate which results in a heat accumulation, and the internal heat accumulation can lead to an increase of temperature to over 300 °C.

claims.claim: Redundant amounts of flame retardant additives can lead to a severe decrease of electrochemical performance, including reduced chemical stability and inhibited ionic conductivity of the electrolyte.
claims.quote: redundant amounts of ﬂame retardants can lead to a severe decrease of electrochemical performance, including reduced chemical stability and inhibited ionic conductivity of the electrolyte.

claims.claim: An electrolyte is considered less flammable when its self-extinguishing time (SET) is between 6 and 20 s/g, and nonflammable when SET is less than 6 s/g.
claims.quote: The retarding eﬃciency of the electrolyte can be considered as less ﬂammable regarding the SET at 6−20 s/g and nonﬂammable at <6 s/g.

claims.claim: Electrochemical polymerization additives cause irreversible damage to battery performance, whereas redox shuttle additives do not cause permanent damage and have little influence on battery performance.
claims.quote: the electrochemical polymerization additives have been used in commercial batteries with good overcharge protection eﬀect, but they can cause irreversible damage to battery performance... redox shuttle additives do not cause permanent damage to the battery and have little inﬂuence on battery performance.

claims.claim: LiPF6 shows poor thermal stability and chemical stability in aqueous systems, making it difficult to meet the needs of high-performance lithium ion batteries despite being the most widely used lithium electrolyte.
claims.quote: LiPF6 is currently the most widely used lithium electrolyte because of its high ionic conductivity, excellent oxidation stability, and low environmental pollution. However, LiPF6 shows poor thermal stability and chemical stability in aqueous systems, which makes it diﬃcult to meet the needs of high-performance lithium ion batteries.

claims.claim: Aqueous lithium ion batteries are limited to a low voltage of less than 1.5 V and low energy density of less than 70 Wh kg⁻¹ due to side reactions including hydrogen/oxygen evolution and electrode material dissolution.
claims.quote: limiting the aqueous lithium ion battery with low voltage (<1.5 V) and low energy density (<70 Wh kg−1).

claims.claim: The sulphide solid electrolyte Li2S–P2S5 glass–ceramic system achieves an ionic conductivity of 0.017 S/cm with a low activation energy of 17 kJ/mol, which is higher than that of Li10GeP2S12.
claims.quote: Hayashi et al. recently reported the solid electrolyte in the Li2S−P2S5 glass−ceramic system with a high ionic conductivity of 0.017 S/cm and low activation energy of 17 kJ/mol, which is higher than that of the previous report of Li10GeP2S12.

claims.claim: PTC electrodes based on current collector modification can respond to temperature rise caused by external abuse such as overcharge and external short circuit, but cannot deal with thermal runaway problems caused by an internal short circuit.
claims.quote: Although the PTC electrodes based on the current collector modiﬁcation can respond to the temperature rise caused by external abuse (such as overcharge and external short circuit), they cannot deal with the thermal runaway problems caused by an internal short circuit.

claims.claim: A thermosensitive electrolyte based on PBMA in EMIM-TFSI ionic liquid showed normal discharge capacity at 60 °C but complete shut-off at 150 °C, which is above the LCST of PBMA-IL.
claims.quote: The battery using the PBMA-IL electrolyte showed a normal discharge capacity at 60 °C, but complete shut-oﬀ occurred as the temperature was 150 °C above the LCST of PBMA-IL.

claims.claim: Replacing the H of alkyl phosphate ester with F results in improved physical and chemical properties including lower melting point, higher flash point, lower viscosity, and more stable electrochemical performance.
claims.quote: If the H of alkyl phosphate ester is replaced by F, its physical and chemical properties will be improved with a lower melting point, higher ﬂash point, lower viscosity, and more stable electrochemical performance.

claims.claim: Among halogen flame retardants, the flame retardancy increases in the order F < Cl < Br < I, but iodine flame retardants are rarely applied because the C–I bond is too weak to maintain stability.
claims.quote: All four halogen elements ﬂuorine (F), chlorine (Cl), bromine (Br), and iodine (I) show ﬂame retardancy in an enhanced order of F, Cl, Br, and I... the iodine ﬂame retardants are randomly applied because the C−I bond in the iodine ﬂame retardant is too weak to maintain stability.

claims.claim: The ISC triggering condition in the model is set as when the temperature of the jellyroll node reaches the separator melting point of 165 °C for the chosen NCA 18650 battery.
claims.quote: The ISC triggering condition is set as when the temperature of the jellyroll node reaches the separator melting point [39, 40] (165 °C for the chosen battery).

claims.claim: Two distinct thermal runaway propagation modes exist, determined by the value of the equivalent thermal resistance θc between batteries, with the threshold at θc = 0.89 K/W.
claims.quote: two modes of TR propagation exist, based on the value of θc... The simulation indicates that the threshold θc = 0.89 K/W.

claims.claim: In Mode I (low θc), local overheating on the battery surface causes the surface temperature to exceed the separator melting point quickly, producing a local short circuit that early-triggers ISC and further accelerates thermal runaway triggering.
claims.quote: Mode I: low θc leads to a local overheating in the battery surface. The surface temperature exceeds the melting point of the separator quickly. The melting of the separator produces a local short circuit. This early-triggered ISC further accelerates TR triggering.

claims.claim: In Mode II (high θc), conductive heat cannot concentrate on the battery surface, the surface and jellyroll average temperatures converge over time, and the total time of TR propagation is significantly longer than in Mode I.
claims.quote: Mode II: high θc prevents conductive heat from concentrating on the battery surface. The surface and jellyroll average temperatures converge over time. When the temperatures reach the TTR (melting point of the separator), the TR will inevitably be triggered. The total time of TR propagation is signiﬁcantly longer than that of Mode I.

claims.claim: For a two-battery pack at d = 0 mm (Mode I), the total TR propagation time Ttotal is approximately 3 s, whereas at d = 0.01 mm (Mode II), Ttotal is prolonged to 18.2 s.
claims.quote: Normally, the Ttotal of a two-battery pack at d = 0 mm is approximately 3 s (Fig. 6a, mode I). For another case at d = 0.01 mm, the Ttotal is prolonged to 18.2 s (Fig. 6b, mode II).

claims.claim: TR propagation speed increases with ambient temperature because a high ambient temperature corresponds to a high initial thermal energy within each battery.
claims.quote: First, the TR propagation speed increases with Tamb, because a high Tamb corresponds to a high initial thermal energy within each battery.

claims.claim: TR propagation speed increases when battery spacing d decreases because decreasing d decreases the equivalent thermal resistance, and lower thermal resistance leads to quicker heat transfer.
claims.quote: Second, the TR propagation speed increases when battery spacing d decreases. Decreasing d means decreasing the equivalent thermal resistance. The lower the thermal resistance, the quicker the heat transfer.

claims.claim: Under Mode I, the TR propagation front within a large pack is approximately circular, whereas under Mode II, the propagation front tends to be a regular polygon (quadrate for 90° packing, hexagon for 60° packing).
claims.quote: Within a large pack, the TR propagation front is approximately a circle based on Mode I, whereas it is a regular polygon based on Mode II... the TR propagation front tends to be quadrate in a 90° pack... In a 60° packing scenario... The TR propagation front tends to be a hexagon.

claims.claim: Battery packs with random heat transfer coefficients between 0 W/K and 2.7 W/K show stable TR propagation that is slightly slower than packs with uniform thermal coefficients, due to uneven thermal contact preventing propagation along a straight line.
claims.quote: Simulation results show that the TR propagations of the battery packs with random thermal coeﬃcients are stable and little slower than the battery packs with even thermal coeﬃcients... Due to the uneven thermal contact condition, the TR cannot always propagation along a straight line, the spatial speed decreases.

claims.claim: Among packing angles of 60°, 75°, and 90°, the α = 60° pack has the fastest TR propagation and the α = 75° pack has the slowest, with the α = 75° pack's slow propagation attributed to low packing density and larger heat dissipation area.
claims.quote: From a quantitative point of view, the TR propagation of the α = 60° pack is the fastest, the α=75° pack is the slowest... For battery packs with α=75°, the thermal contact condition is the same as packs with α=90°, however, their packing density is low, and the heat dissipation area is larger.

claims.claim: Under the same overcharge condition (1C CC overcharge from 100% SOC), the LFP Li-ion cell experienced thermal runaway first at 1001 s, while NCM811, NCM622, and NCM111 cells reached TR at 1229 s, 1450 s, and 1982 s, respectively.
claims.quote: LFP Li-ion cell first reached the point in time when TR occurs (tTR) (approximately 1001 s), while the values of tTR of the NCM811, NCM622 and NCM111 Li-ion cells are 1229s, 1450 s and 1982 s, respectively.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature during thermal runaway of the LFP Li-ion cell (308 °C) was less than half that of any NCM Li-ion cell, with NCM811 reaching 695 °C, NCM622 reaching 629 °C, and NCM111 reaching 589 °C.
claims.quote: The Tmax of the LFP Li-ion cell is 308 °C, less than half of that of NCM Li-ion cells... Tmax:308°C... Tmax:695°C... Tmax:629 °C... Tmax:589°C

claims.claim: All three NCM Li-ion cells (NCM811, NCM622, NCM111) caught fire or exploded during thermal runaway, while the LFP Li-ion cell only released a large amount of smoke without fire.
claims.quote: All NCM Li-ion cells caught fire or exploded during TR, while the LFP Li-ion cell only released a large amount of smoke without fire.

claims.claim: The onset temperature of thermal runaway (Tonset) for the LFP Li-ion cell was 112 °C, which is higher than the Tonset of approximately 92–93 °C observed for all three NCM Li-ion cells.
claims.quote: the temperature of LFP Li-ion cell increases from Tc (Tc=55 °C) to the onset temperature of TR (Tonset =112 °C), while the Tonset of NCM Li-ion cells is about 92 °C.

claims.claim: For NCM Li-ion cells, as the proportion of nickel in the cathode material increases, the accumulated heat (AH) during thermal runaway increases, indicating a positive correlation between nickel content and thermal hazards.
claims.quote: For NCM Li-ion cells, as the proportion of Ni in the cathode electrode active material increases, the value of AH rises, which indicates that when TR occurs, the higher the proportion of Ni in cathode active materials, the higher the thermal hazards.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature rate (Rmax) during thermal runaway was 118.6 °C s−1 for NCM622, 98.2 °C s−1 for NCM811, 81.7 °C s−1 for NCM111, and only 15.7 °C s−1 for LFP, making LFP the least thermally hazardous.
claims.quote: the maximum value of Rmax is 118.6 °C s−1, which appears on the temperature rate vs. temperature plot of LMN622 Li-ion cell during TR... The value of Rmax of LFP Li-ion cell is the smallest among four Li-ion cells, only 15.7 °C s−1.

claims.claim: The SEI film decomposes exothermically and generates gases (CO2, O2, C2H4) when cell temperature exceeds 90 °C, after which intercalated lithium reacts with electrolyte without SEI protection.
claims.quote: When the temperature exceeds 90 °C, the metastable Solid Electrolyte Interface (SEI) film will exothermically decompose and generate various gases (such as CO2, O2 and C2H4), and then the reactions between intercalated lithium and electrolyte occur without the protection of SEI film.

claims.claim: Once cell temperature exceeds 60 °C during overcharge, various side reactions are accelerated, and the acceleration of side reactions further increases cell temperature in a self-reinforcing manner.
claims.quote: Once the cell temperature exceeds 60 °C, various side reactions will be accelerated. At the same time, the acceleration of the side reactions will further increase the cell temperature.

claims.claim: The time interval (tint) between the sharp rise of cell voltage and the onset of thermal runaway was approximately 189 s for LFP, but at least two minutes shorter for NCM cells (65 s for NCM811, 21 s for NCM622, 15 s for NCM111).
claims.quote: The tint of LFP Li-ion cell is about 189 s, which is enough for BMS and the drivers to response and cut off the current. However, the tint of NCM Li-ion cells is at least two minutes shorter than LFP Li-ion cell.

claims.claim: The LFP cathode material does not generate oxygen during decomposition at high temperatures due to the high-energy P=O bond in (PO4)3−, which prevents structural collapse and formation of strong oxidizing substances, resulting in a lower peak temperature during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: For LFP Li-ion cell, the O2 is not easily released when the decomposition reaction of cathode occurs due to the existence of high energy P=O bond of (PO4)3− with in LFP cathode material. As a result, the P−O bond in the crystal of LFP cathode material is stable and difficult to be broken, so it is not easy to collapse or form a strong oxidizing substance even under overcharge condition, thus resulting in a lower peak temperature.

claims.claim: The mass loss ratio after thermal runaway followed the order NCM811 (44.3%) > NCM622 (42.6%) > NCM111 (38.7%) > LFP (17.7%), with NCM811 and NCM622 exceeding 40% mass loss.
claims.quote: the mass loss ratio is in the order of NCM811> NCM622>NCM111>LFP... The mass loss ratio of LFP Li-ion cell is only 17.7%... the mass loss ratio of NCM811 and NCM622 even exceeds 40% after TR.

claims.claim: The standard commercial electrolyte (1M LiPF6/EC:DEC:DMC=1:1:1) can be oxidized in the voltage range of 4.9–5 V, releasing CH4 and other alkyl radicals that increase electrode spacing.
claims.quote: Kumai et al. have proved that the standard commercial electrolyte (1M LiPF6/EC:DEC:DMC=1:1:1 in volume) can be oxidized in the voltage range of 4.9–5 V, which will release CH4 and other alkyl radicals into the package of the cell, resulting in the increase in the distance between electrodes.

claims.claim: For the Typ1 cell (NCM442/graphite/PE+ceramic), thermal runaway was triggered at 247°C with a maximum temperature of 866.3°C under adiabatic ARC conditions at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: At 247 °C, temperature rises sharply to 866.3 °C once thermal runaway occurred.

claims.claim: For the Typ2 cell (LFP/MCMB/PP+ceramic), thermal runaway occurred at 208.8°C with a maximum temperature of 367.9°C under adiabatic ARC conditions at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: At 208.8 °C, temperature rises steeply with thermal runaway happening and then reaches 367.9 °C, which is the maximum temperature during the test.

claims.claim: For the Typ3 cell (NCM111+LMO/MAG10/PE without ceramic coating), self-heating was detected at 85°C, which is lower than the Typ1 and Typ2 cells, due to the lower onset temperature of the MAG10 anode.
claims.quote: The self-heating was detected at 85 °C... The exothermic reaction of anode began at 85 °C, which is lower than that of the Typ1 and Typ2 cells, indicating that the heat generation in Stage I for the Typ3 cell was more eminent.

claims.claim: For the Typ4 cell (LCO/LTO/PE+ceramic), no internal short circuit occurred during ARC testing because the separator collapse temperature (~290°C) exceeded the full decomposition temperature of the cathode and anode, leaving no active material to sustain a short circuit.
claims.quote: According to the DSC test results, there was no internal short circuit of the Typ4 cell during ARC test, because the collapse of the separator occurred at 290 °C (as shown in Fig. 5(i)), which is much higher than the full decomposition temperature of the cathode and anode. In other words, no active material left means no internal short circuit.

claims.claim: For the Typ1 and Typ3 cells, where internal short circuit triggered intensive exothermic reactions, the total heat released (ΔH) exceeded the 100% charged electric energy (ΔHele), with η = 160.87% and 113.44% respectively; whereas for Typ2 and Typ4 cells with gentle or no internal short circuit, ΔH was lower than ΔHele, with η = 62.03% and 62.09% respectively.
claims.quote: The total heat released ΔH was larger than the 100% charged electric energy ΔHele for the Typ1 and Typ3 cells. On the contrary, the internal short circuit for the Typ2 cell is quite gentle, and there is no internal short circuit for the Typ4 cell. Consequently, the total heat released ΔH was lower than the 100% charged electric energy ΔHele for the Typ2 and Typ4 cells.

claims.claim: Internal short circuit releases extra electrical heat comparable in magnitude to the heat released by chemical reactions, making it a key determinant of thermal runaway severity.
claims.quote: The results and analysis indicate that the severity of thermal runaway is highly related with internal short circuit, because internal short circuit releases extra electrical heat, which is comparable with the heat released by chemical reactions.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway can occur without internal short circuit, as demonstrated by the Typ4 cell (LCO/LTO), which underwent thermal runaway driven solely by exothermic reactions of cathode and anode materials with electrolyte.
claims.quote: Last but not least, the occurrence of the internal short circuit is not equivalent with the triggering of thermal runaway. Sometimes there is thermal runaway (for the Typ4 cell), but with no internal short circuit.

claims.claim: For the Typ3 cell, the absence of ceramic coating on the PE separator caused the separator to immediately collapse after shrinkage, leading to full-scale internal short circuit and voltage drop to 0V in a short time.
claims.quote: Without ceramic coating, the voltage dropped to 0 V in a short time after the separator melting, indicating a full-scale internal short circuit at this stage.

claims.claim: The NCA anode (LTO) has a DSC onset temperature of approximately 140°C and enthalpy of 383 J/g, whereas MCMB graphite has an onset temperature of approximately 125°C and enthalpy of 2222 J/g, both measured with electrolyte at 100% SOC and 10°C/min scan rate.

Residual analysis of Typ1 and Typ3 cells after ARC testing showed no aluminum electrodes, indicating that the maximum internal temperature exceeded 660°C (the melting point of aluminum) in these tests.
claims.quote: Inside the cells, there were no aluminum electrode observed inside the residuals of Typ1 and Typ3 cells, indicating that the maximum internal temperature exceeds 660 °C in these tests.

claims.claim: Electric short circuit is a necessary but not sufficient condition for the occurrence of thermal runaway after mechanical abuse of a lithium-ion battery.
claims.quote: electric short circuit is a necessary but not sufficient condition for the occurrence of thermal runaway after mechanical abuse.

claims.claim: The separator in lithium-ion battery construction has a melting point of around 144°C, which is lower than other cell layers.
claims.quote: as the only separators have the lower melting point in lithium-ion battery construction, it can melt at around 144°C [33], so separator failure will occur earlier compared to other layers' failure which have comparatively high temperatures.

claims.claim: In the three-point bend test simulation, a sudden drop in force at around 7mm displacement indicates the point of short circuit occurrence.
claims.quote: force attains the peak value for three-point bend, however sudden drop in force at around 7mm shows the point of short circuit occurrence.

claims.claim: After internal electrochemical failures during three-point bend loading, temperatures of all separator layers reached around 300°C, except sep-05 to sep-07 which attained temperatures of around 500°C for a short instance.
claims.quote: temperature of all layers were around 300°C except sep-05 to sep-07 which attain temperatures of around 500°C for a short instance of time.

claims.claim: In the three-point bend test, separator failure occurs due to brittle fracture immediately after steel casing fracture, caused by combined tension and compression acting on the separator layer with low mechanical strength.
claims.quote: Due to both tension and compression separator layer with low mechanical strength develops brittle fracture, which occurs immediately after steel casing fracture and temperature increase drastically.

claims.claim: The three-point bend test produces an immediate short circuit response, whereas the circular punch test shows a slow build-up of short circuit, but in both cases separator failure occurs well in advance of short circuit.
claims.quote: In the case of three-point bend test which has immediate short circuit response and circular punch test where a slow build-up of short circuit is evident from experiment, separator failure occurs well in advance for short circuit.

claims.claim: Internal separator layers are more thermally active than outer layers during compression loading due to forces from the rigid bottom plate, heat transfer inside the cell, and high compression rate.
claims.quote: internal separator layers are more thermally active, which is due to the variety of reasons including forces applied from the rigid bottom plate, heat transfer inside the cell and high compression rate compared to other layers.

claims.claim: The safety valve opening (SVO) detected a minor overpressure (PSVO) whose maximum value ranged from 2.8 to 4.3 kPa, and this value was independent of the SOC or the external heating power of the LIB.
claims.quote: the SVO detected a minor overpressure (PSVO) whose maximum value ranged from 2.8 to 4.3 kPa in the two test sets. It was independent from the SOC or the external heating power of the LIB.

claims.claim: At 100% SOC and 400 W heating power, the maximum impact pressure during the intense ejection stage reached 107.2 kPa, while at 20% SOC and 400 W it was only 10.8 kPa.
claims.quote: The ejection of the thermal runaway generated maximum overpressures of 10.8, 11.9, and 19.2 kPa when the battery SOC was of 20%, 40%, and 60%, respectively. However, thermal runaway occurred dramatically at a high SOC with the maximum impact pressure soaring to 86.5 and 107.2 kPa when the battery SOCs were 80% and 100%, respectively.

claims.claim: At 100% SOC and 400 W heating power, the maximum pressure-rise rate during the intense ejection stage was 694.4 kPa·s⁻¹, compared to only 64.6 kPa·s⁻¹ at 40% SOC and 400 W.
claims.quote: the maximum rates soared to 673.6 and 694.4 kPa∙s−1 and the minimum rates were −350.9 and −486.4 kPa∙s−1 with an 80% and 100% SOC, respectively. The maximum pressure-rise rates were 42.6, 64.6, and 49.2 kPa∙s−1...when the thermal runaway occurred at a 20%, 40%, and 60% SOC, respectively.

claims.claim: The time interval between the safety valve opening and the intense ejection stage (tSVO-IES) ranged from 184.4 to 568.8 s, providing sufficient time to shut down the battery circuit or start the cooling system.
claims.quote: the time interval between the SVO and the IES (tSVO-IES) ranged from 184.4 to 568.8 s in this work and provided sufficient time to shut down the battery circuit or to start the cooling system.

claims.claim: At 400 W heating power, the initial pressure rise in the intense ejection stage occurred 0.4 to 3.8 s before the initial rapid temperature rise across SOCs of 20%–100%, whereas at lower heating powers of 200–350 W the initial pressure rise occurred 8.5 to 45.6 s after the initial rapid temperature rise.
claims.quote: the initial pressure rise occurred 0.4, 3.8, 2.4, 2.2, and 2.1 s before the initial rapid temperature rise when the battery was at 20%, 40%, 60%, 80%, and 100% SOC, respectively. The initial pressure rise occurred 8.5, 45.6, 17.6, and 13.7 s after the initial rapid temperature rise when the battery heating power was 200, 250, 300, and 350 W, respectively.

claims.claim: A single 18650 LIB thermal runaway near the thorax of a human in a semi-closed space yields an accident fatality rate close to 10%, based on Bowen curve analysis of the measured impact pressure and duration.
claims.quote: If thermal runaway occurs in a single 18650 LIB near the thorax of a human in a semi-closed space, the accident fatality rate is close to 10%.

claims.claim: A 0% SOC battery at 400 W heating power and a 100% SOC battery at 100 W heating power both produced only the SVO minor pressure with no thermal runaway or intense major impact pressure.
claims.quote: Tests of a 0% SOC battery with 400-W heating power and a 100% SOC battery with 100-W heating power were also conducted, but both had only the SVO minor pressure and no thermal runaway or intense major impact pressure.

claims.claim: The exothermic onset temperature of the SAMSUNG S5 HPLIB was measured to be as low as 117.0 °C, making it the lowest among the seven studied smart phone batteries.
claims.quote: SAMSUNG S5 shall carry the most unstable feature with an exothermic onset temperature as low as 117 °C.

claims.claim: The SAMSUNG Note 3 HPLIB reached a maximum temperature of 675.6 °C and a maximum self-heat rate of 11,860.0 °C min⁻¹ during thermal runaway, representing the worst-case scenario among the tested batteries.
claims.quote: SAMSUNG Note 3 displays the worst-case scenario by possessing the maximum temperature and maximum self-heat rate reaching the extremity of 675.6 °C and 11,860.0 °C min-1.

claims.claim: The crucial temperature (Tcr) is defined as the point at which self-heat rate jumps to 100 °C min⁻¹ or higher, beyond which thermal runaway becomes irreversible and cannot be stopped by any mitigation method.
claims.quote: a crucial temperature (Tcr) is selected at the point with a temperature jump or a self-heat rate jumping to 100 °C min-1 or even a higher value... in case of the runaway reaction penetrates through the crucial point, this uncontrollably wicked behavior cannot be obstructed by any means.

claims.claim: All seven tested HPLIBs reached maximum temperatures exceeding the auto-ignition temperatures of EC (465 °C) and DEC (445 °C), meaning released electrolytes will automatically catch fire when mixed with the atmosphere.
claims.quote: the maximum temperatures of the HPLIBs under thermal runaway all exceed the AITs of EC and DEC at 465 and 445 °C, respectively; it is worth pointing out that the HPLIBs or released electrolytes will automatically catch fire if the superheated effluents released from HPLIBs and mixed with the atmosphere.

claims.claim: Under 2 atm nitrogen backpressure, the iPhone 6 HPLIB still underwent thermal runaway with onset temperature 174.3 °C, maximum temperature 451.0 °C, and maximum self-heat rate 3419.0 °C min⁻¹, demonstrating that elevated nitrogen pressure does not prevent thermal runaway.
claims.quote: It was surprising that there were still keeping thermal runaway of the HPLIB under confinement test at elevated temperature exceeding 174 °C. Under the stagnation pressure of nitrogen gas, the onset temperature, crucial temperature, maximum temperature, and maximum self-heat rate are determined to be 174.3 °C (lowest of TC2), 189.1 °C (lowest TC1), 451.0 °C (highest of TC1) and 3419.0 °C min-1 (highest of TC2), respectively.

claims.claim: The averaged maximum self-heat rate of the tested HPLIBs (4993.0 °C min⁻¹) is much lower than the averaged maximum self-heat rate of 18650 cylindrical lithium-ion batteries (approximately 11,413.0 °C min⁻¹).
claims.quote: the averaged maximum self-heat rate is 4993.0 °C min-1 which is much lower than that of 18650 lithium-ion batteries with an averaged value of about 11,413.0 °C min-1.

claims.claim: The transition from crucial temperature to the point of maximum self-heat rate occurs in less than 5 seconds, during which the battery temperature remains nearly constant, attributed to collapse of the separator causing internal Al-anode short circuit.
claims.quote: it took on a few seconds (less than 5 s) from Tcr to the point with maximum self-heat rate (Tmax-rate). However, in this instant the battery keeps almost constant temperature, which has been proclaimed to be the collapse of separator, thus causing the internal Al-anode short circuit and accelerating the violent heat-up by Joule effect.

claims.claim: The crucial temperatures of SAMSUNG Note 3 and S5 HPLIBs were 167.6 °C and 130.6 °C respectively, which are much lower than the 182–213 °C range observed for commercial 18650 lithium-ion batteries.
claims.quote: Appearing the other way about crucial temperatures of HPLIB in SAMSUNG Note 3 and S5 are, respectively, detected to be 167.6 and 130.6 °C, which emerge much lower crucial temperatures than those of 18650 lithium-ion batteries.

claims.claim: Nickel (Ni) ion content in cathode materials of LiMO2 increases battery capacity but destabilizes the battery with a lower exothermic onset temperature, representing a trade-off between capacity and safety.
claims.quote: Ni content in cathode materials of LiMO2 could increase the capacity of LIB but destabilized the stability of LIB with a lower exothermic onset temperature... Ni ion in cathode materials can increase capacity in LIB at the sacrifice of safety.

claims.claim: HPLIBs used in smart phones lack a central support structure (unlike cylindrical 18650 cells) and do not have a rupture disk for pressure relief, making them more prone to mechanical or thermal abuse.
claims.quote: the HPLIB lacks of a central support in a cylindrical cell such as commercial 18650 LIB. Besides, a cylindrical LIB is equipped with a rupture disk to relieve at set pressure for avoiding the overpressure of the LIB. Smart phones do not have such dually protective devices because thickness reduction is a desire for most customers.

claims.claim: After mechanical indentation tests approaching the onset of internal short circuit, Li-ion cell capacity reduced by 0.5%–6% of its original value across three cell capacities (500, 1500, and 2000 mAh).
claims.quote: After indentation test, the cell capacity reduced by 0.5%–6% of its original value.

claims.claim: Mechanical indentation results in a reduction in ohmic resistance and an increase in polarization resistance, as determined by EIS fitting.
claims.quote: the fitting results of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) indicated that mechanical indentation can result in a reduction in ohmic resistance and the increase in polarization resistance.

claims.claim: Under mechanical indentation with a 12 mm indenter, cells with larger capacities can withstand greater deformation loads before onset of internal short circuit; the maximum load at ISCr was 4771.7 N for 500 mAh, 5042.3 N for 1500 mAh, and 6202.4 N for 2000 mAh cells.
claims.quote: The Loadmax of shorted cell with a capacity of 500 mAh was 4771.7 N and 5024.3 N for 1500 mAh cell... As a result, under the same mechanical indentation condition, the cells with larger capacities have a greater tolerance to load.

claims.claim: The decrease in ohmic resistance under indentation conditions is attributed to expansion of effective current collector area due to tension from the indenter, reduced electrode spacing, and possible plastic deformation of the separator rather than electrolyte changes.
claims.quote: the decrease in Rohm can be mainly attributed to the changes in current collectors. On the one hand, the current collectors were stretched due to the existence of tension that the indenter applied on them, leading to the expansion of effective area of the current collectors. On the other hand, the distance between electrodes also reduced.

claims.claim: SEM examination revealed that graphite anode particles at the indentation area had sharp edges and corners crushed away, with micro-pores and aisles blocked due to particle collapse, while graphite particles away from the indentation area remained unchanged.
claims.quote: the sharp edges and corners of the plate-like graphite particles disappeared at the indentation area, and the graphite particles were also crushed... The micro-pores and the aisle in the anode were blocked due to the collapse of graphite particles, thus resulting in the increase in cell polarization resistance... away from the indentation area, the granular graphite particles and the porosity of also remain unchanged.

claims.claim: Cathode particles (LiCoO2) showed no distinct cracks under mechanical indentation conditions, indicating strong compressive strength of cobalt-based oxide, while the cathode surface was flattened at the indentation area.
claims.quote: the particles of cathode were intact, and no distinct cracks were observed, indicating the strong compressive strength of cobalt-based oxide... the stereoscopic effect of surface morphology of the indentation area is weaker than that of the area away from the indentation, manifesting that the cathode electrode surface is flattened as well.

claims.claim: Separator pores were significantly enlarged after indentation, changing shape from small openings in weaved fibers to larger circles and ovals, and the separator became thinner, which is proposed to contribute to reduced ohmic resistance.
claims.quote: the pores in the separator were significantly enlarged after indentation test, and the shape of pores also changed from small openings in weaved fibers to much larger circles and ovals... the reduction of separator thickness is one of the reasons for reducing the ohmic resistance (Rohm).

claims.claim: The effect of loss of lithium inventory (LLI) was not observed from IC curve evolution in single indentation tests, but was observed in repeated indentation tests on the 2000 mAh cell at around 3.8 V in the IC curves.
claims.quote: The effects of loss of lithium inventory (LLI) was not observed from the evolution of IC curves before and after indentation tests... some changes related to other degradation mechanisms were observed in the IC curves of 2000 mAh cell at around 3.8 V in Fig. 4 (d). This phenomenon can be explained by the effect of LLI.

claims.claim: A 500 mAh cell indented with an 8 mm indenter showed no reduction in discharge capacity, whereas 1500 mAh and 2000 mAh cells showed observable capacity loss of 0.5%–6% after indentation.
claims.quote: after performing the indentation test with an 8 mm indenter, the discharge capacity of 500mAh cell showed no reduction... observable capacity loss appeared after indentation tests for 1500 mAh and 2000 mAh cells, reducing by 0.5%–6% of their original capacity.

claims.claim: Mud cracks and ductile fractures observed in copper current collectors by XCT can isolate active materials coated on them and block the transport path of Li-ions, thereby increasing cell internal resistance.
claims.quote: Cracks (or fractures) on the copper foil may isolate the active materials that coated on them, causing the emergence of inactive portion and blocking the transport path of fuel (i.e. Li-ions). It is therefore conceivable that cell internal resistance would rise.

claims.claim: The ductile fracture sequence under indentation initiates with shear band extension in granular electrode coating, followed by shear offset and crack initiation, then copper foil fracture, then multi-layer cracking, then aluminum foil fracture, and finally separator failure leading to internal short circuit.
claims.quote: The failure process starts with the extension of shear band in the granular coating (i.e. active materials), and shear offset and cracks initiate and develop from the shear band... the copper foil tends to fracture first followed by multi-layer cracking and faulting... the ductile fracture in aluminum current collector occurs later than the ductile fracture in cooper current collector... the separator layer failed due to the intrusion of deformation-hardened electrodes, leading to the direct contact between electrodes with opposite polarity, thus creating an internal short.

claims.claim: A 500 mAh cell experiencing a soft-short under mechanical indentation showed a surface temperature rise rate of approximately 0.62 °C/s in the initial stage, while a 1500 mAh cell experiencing a hard-short showed a temperature rise rate of approximately 1.37 °C/s.
claims.quote: the cell temperature rose rapidly from ambient temperature, 23.9 °C, to 31.9 °C within 13 s, the temperature rate was about 0.62 °C·s−1... the temperature rate was about 1.37 °C·s−1. This a typical behavior of a hard-short under large discharge current.

claims.claim: Water mist reduced the peak average temperature of the cell long surface by 133 °C compared to the case without extinguishing agent, while CO2 reduced it by 43 °C and HFC-227ea by 75 °C, before the respective agents were depleted.
claims.quote: the peak average temperatures before the extinguishing agents were depleted were 43, 75 and 133 °C lower, respectively, than that in the situation without an extinguishing agent.

claims.claim: During release of CO2 and HFC-227ea, a flame was observed near the negative tab of the cell, whereas no flame was observed during the release of water mist.
claims.quote: a flame is observed during the release of CO2 and HFC-227ea, while no flame is observed in the case with water mist, which means that water mist shows a better suppressive effect than the other two extinguishing agents.

claims.claim: The peak upper surface temperature (T4) of the NCM/graphite cell without extinguishing agent was 621 °C, while with CO2 it was 465 °C, with HFC-227ea 479 °C, and with water mist 427 °C.
claims.quote: The Tp in case 1 was 621 °C, while that in cases 2 and 3 were similar with 465 °C and 479 °C. However, the Tp in case 4 was 427 °C, which was lower than that in cases 2 and 3.

claims.claim: During water mist release, the temperature rise rate (dT/dt) of the upper surface was negative (−1.23 °C/s), indicating that heat absorbed by water mist exceeded heat released by the cell; in contrast, CO2 and HFC-227ea yielded positive temperature rise rates of 1.86 °C/s and 1.41 °C/s respectively.
claims.quote: the dT/dt in case 4 was negative, indicating that the heat absorbed by the water mist was more than the heat released from the cell during the whole period of releasing water... the dT/dt in cases 2 and 3 were both positive.

claims.claim: The temperature above the safety valve (T6) peaked at 108 °C with water mist, compared to 660 °C without extinguishing agent, 306 °C with CO2, and 471 °C with HFC-227ea.
claims.quote: the temperature of T6 in case 4 peaked at 108 °C, while that in cases 1–3 were 660 °C, 306 °C and 471 °C, respectively, which further proved that water mist possesses the best cooling effect compared to the other two extinguishing agents.

claims.claim: Safety venting of the 94 Ah NCM/graphite cell occurred when the cell surface temperature reached a critical range of approximately 101–151 °C, after which thermal runaway was triggered about 7 seconds later.
claims.quote: When the cell surface temperature reached at a critical temperature (nearly 101–151 °C) at 859 s, the safety venting occurred. And thermal runaway was triggered about 7 s after the safety venting.

claims.claim: The latent heat of vaporization of CO2, HFC-227ea, and water mist used as extinguishing agents is 119.37, 132.6, and 2266 kJ/kg·K respectively.
claims.quote: The latent heat of vaporization of different extinguishing agents in cases 2–4 is 119.37, 132.6 and 2266 kJ/kg·K, respectively.

claims.claim: Heat absorbed by decomposition (Qd) of the extinguishing agent occurs only in the HFC-227ea case, not in the CO2 or water mist cases.
claims.quote: All cases contain Qv and Qnr. However, Qd only exists in case 3.

claims.claim: After the extinguishing agent was depleted, cell temperatures rose again because cooling then relied solely on natural convection, indicating that sustained agent application is required to prevent re-heating.
claims.quote: Once the agent was depleted, the cooling rate of the cell drops. At this moment, the cell temperature rose again. This is due to the high temperature of the cell. And the cell cooling mainly relied on natural cooling after the agent was depleted.

claims.claim: Without any extinguishing agent, the 94 Ah NCM/graphite cell exhibited a temperature rise rate of approximately 4.54 °C/s after safety venting during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the temperature rising rate after the safety venting in case 1 was nearly 4.54 °C/s, which was still far greater than that in cases 2 and 3.

claims.claim: For fully charged 50 Ah NCM/graphite LIBs, thermal runaway can be avoided by heat dissipation before the surface temperature exceeds 126.5–132.5 °C.
claims.quote: Thermal runaway can be avoided by heat dissipation before temperature beyond 126.5-132.5 ℃.

claims.claim: The onset temperature of thermal runaway for a 50 Ah LIB heated with a cylindrical heater was lower than for the same battery heated with an electric furnace.
claims.quote: The onset temperature of thermal runaway for the battery heated with a cylindrical heater was lower than for the battery heated with an electric furnace.

claims.claim: The battery heated with an electric furnace produced a larger explosion and more jet fire compared to the battery heated with a cylindrical heater.
claims.quote: the battery heated with an electric furnace produced a larger explosion and more jet fire.

claims.claim: More sparks and gas/white smoke ejection were observed for the battery heated with a cylindrical heater, whereas the battery heated with an electric furnace exhibited more jet flame.
claims.quote: More sparks and gas/smoke ejection could be observed for the battery heated with a cylindrical heater, while the battery heated with an electric furnace produced a larger explosion and more jet fire.

claims.claim: Heat release, CO2 production, and mass loss increased with increasing heating power or heating area for the tested 50 Ah LIBs.
claims.quote: The heat release, CO2 production, and mass loss were found increase as the increasing heating power or heating area.

claims.claim: The impact of heating position on battery burning was less obvious compared with heating power and heating area.
claims.quote: the impact of heating position on battery burning was less obvious compared with heating power and heating area.

claims.claim: When the surface temperature of a fully charged 50 Ah LIB reached 120–140 °C, thermal runaway was triggered, accompanied by audible sound and bright sparks.
claims.quote: Subsequently, when the surface temperature of the LIB reached 120-140 ℃, thermal runaway was triggered, an audible sound and bright sparks were witnessed.

claims.claim: The maximum surface temperature during thermal abuse of the tested 50 Ah LIBs ranged from 570 to 660 °C across all experiments.
claims.quote: The value of maximum surface temperature was 570-660 ℃ for the battery during whole experiments.

claims.claim: The average peak HRR values were 27.30, 22.36, 34.53, and 37.08 kW for tests 1, 2, 3, and 4 (cylindrical heater side, cylindrical heater bottom, 0.4 kW electric furnace bottom, and 2.0 kW electric furnace bottom), respectively.
claims.quote: The average HRR peak values were about 27.30, 22.36, 34.53 and 37.08 kW for test 1, test 2, test 3 and test 4, respectively.

claims.claim: The total heat release (THR) values were 613.8, 538.4, 755.1, and 1059.6 kJ for the cylindrical heater at side, cylindrical heater at bottom, 0.4 kW electric furnace at bottom, and 2.0 kW electric furnace at bottom, respectively.
claims.quote: the average total heat release (THR) values corresponding to the integral HRR were 613.8, 538.4, 755.1 and 1059.6 kJ, respectively.

claims.claim: The battery heated with a cylindrical heater released approximately 1.33–2.12 times more toxic gas (CO) than the battery heated with the electric furnace.
claims.quote: the LIB heated with the cylindrical heater released approximately 1.33-2.12 times more toxic gas than the LIB heated with the electric furnace.

claims.claim: The mass loss during the second stage (violent ejection and combustion) accounted for more than 90% of the total mass loss for the tested LIBs.
claims.quote: The mass loss during the second stage accounted for more than 90 % of the total mass loss.

claims.claim: Total mass loss values were 214.2, 202.8, 259.3, and 275.4 g for the batteries heated with cylindrical heater at side, cylindrical heater at bottom, 0.4 kW electric furnace at bottom, and 2.0 kW electric furnace at bottom, respectively.
claims.quote: The values of total mass loss were 214.2, 202.8, 259.3 and 275.4 g for a battery heated with the cylindrical heater (at side and bottom) and electric furnace (with 0.4 kW and 2.0 kW), respectively.

claims.claim: The LIB heated with the electric furnace exhibited a greater heat release, approximately 1.23–1.73 times more energy than the LIB heated with the cylindrical heater.
claims.quote: the LIB heated with the electric furnace exhibited a greater heat release, releasing approximately 1.23-1.73 times more energy.

claims.claim: The coagulation and sintering of cathode residue under electric furnace heating conditions was attributed to the high-temperature flame environment, consistent with the higher reaction intensity observed under those conditions.
claims.quote: the coagulation and sintering of cathode residue under electric furnace conditions occurs due to the high-temperature flame environment, which is consistent with the reaction intensity and burning behavior mentioned previously.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway onset temperatures were 126.5, 128.3, 130.1, and 132.5 °C for tests 1 (cylindrical heater side), 2 (cylindrical heater bottom), 3 (0.4 kW electric furnace bottom), and 4 (2.0 kW electric furnace bottom), respectively.
claims.quote: Onset temperature/℃ ... 126.5 [test 1] ... 128.3 [test 2] ... 130.1 [test 3] ... 132.5 [test 4]

claims.claim: A battery presents a high thermal abuse hazard under the heating method with higher heating power or larger heating area, such as the tested electric furnace conditions.
claims.quote: a battery presents a high thermal abuse hazard under the heating method with higher heating power or larger heating area, namely, the tested electric furnace conditions in this paper.

claims.claim: When one cell in a 4-series 2-parallel battery pack is externally short-circuited during pack discharging, cells in the branch without the ESC cell experience severe continuous discharging while cells in the other branch (containing the ESC cell) are charged to balance the voltages of the two branches.
claims.quote: no matter a battery pack is charged or discharged, when one cell in the pack is short-circuited, cells in the branch without the ESC cell experience severe continuous discharging while cells in the other branch are charged to balance the voltages of the two branches.

claims.claim: During pack-level ESC fault under discharging condition, cells in the branch without the ESC cell can be subject to over-discharge fault, while during charging condition, cells in the branch containing the ESC cell can be subject to over-charge fault.
claims.quote: cells in the branch without the ESC cell can be subject to over-discharge fault when the whole battery pack is being discharged, and cells in the branch with the ESC cell can be subject to over-charge fault when the whole battery pack is being charged.

claims.claim: The surface temperature of the ESC cell (cell 02) reached a maximum of approximately 110 °C during a pack-level external short circuit under 4.8 A constant current discharging condition starting from approximately 56% SoC.
claims.quote: The ﬁrst set is just cell 02 which is subject to ESC fault. Its surface temperature went up drastically within a short time after ESC occurred... The maximum temperature was around 110 °C.

claims.claim: Cell 05, which experienced the most severe over-discharging in the non-ESC branch during pack discharging ESC fault, reached a maximum surface temperature of more than 70 °C.
claims.quote: Particularly, cell 05 reached a maximum temperature of more than 70 °C since it experienced the most seriously over discharging condition.

claims.claim: Under ESC conditions, SEI decomposition represents the largest part of the heat generated by side reactions and occurs before negative-solvent reaction as temperature rises.
claims.quote: In the both two groups under constant current charging and constant power discharging, SEI decomposition represents the largest part of the heat generated by side reactions. It is reasonable because when the battery experiences temperature rising under the ESC condition, the SEI decomposition occurs before negative-solvent reaction.

claims.claim: The ANN-based current prediction method achieved an RMSE of 3.72 A (3.4% of maximum short-circuit current) under constant current charging and 6.61 A (5.8% of maximum current) under constant power discharging using only voltage information without current sensors.
claims.quote: The RMSE between the estimated and measured current is 3.72 A under constant current charging condition and 6.61 A under constant power discharging condition.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature estimation error using the 3D electro-thermal model was 4.9 °C (4.5% of maximum temperature) under 4.8 A constant current charging and 7.3 °C (7.2% of maximum temperature) under 75 W constant power discharging, achieved without current or temperature sensors.
claims.quote: The maximum error between estimation and measurement is 4.9 °C under 4.8 A constant current charging, which accounts for only 4.5% of the maximum temperature. Under 75 W constant power discharging, the maximum error is 7.3 °C, or 7.2% of the maximum temperature.

claims.claim: In battery packs, the cell-to-temperature-sensor ratio is often as low as 10:1, making direct temperature measurement of every cell impractical.
claims.quote: In general, there are very few temperature sensors in a battery module, and the cell-to-sensor ratio is often as low as 10:1.

claims.claim: The reversible entropic heat term in the heat generation equation is much smaller than the Joule heat term especially under high current conditions typical of ESC faults.
claims.quote: The second term is much smaller than the ﬁrst one especially under high current, which is typical of ESC fault.

claims.claim: During ESC fault, as the short circuit continues, the temperature rise spreads from the inside of the battery to the surface and the temperature near the negative tab is slightly higher than the temperature near the positive tab.
claims.quote: as the ESC continues, the temperature rise spreads rapidly from the inside of the battery to the surface and the temperature near the negative tab is slightly higher than the temperature near the positive tab.

claims.claim: A warning notification was issued on average after 15% of the overcharge time, and an E-stop was triggered after 35% of total overcharge duration before failure.
claims.quote: On average, a warning notification was issued after 15% of the overcharge time, and an E-stop was triggered after 35% of total overcharge duration before failure.

claims.claim: Gas sensing methods can only detect thermal runaway events in progress and could provide a mere 30 s warning before a hazardous event.
claims.quote: Such methods only detect thermal runaway events in progress and therefore could provide a mere 30 s warning, requiring extremely rapid response that is often not practical.

claims.claim: Initial gas release during overcharge occurs at around 120% SoC and is anaerobic with increasing pressure but is difficult to detect; it is not until a cell vents that the hazardous event may be detected.
claims.quote: Gas sensing is widely applied to various electrochemical energy storage solutions, however initial gas release at around 120% SoC is anaerobic with increasing pressure but is difficult to detect. It is not until a cell vents that this hazardous event may be detected.

claims.claim: High temperature CV overcharge (65°C) caused failures an order of magnitude faster than room temperature CV overcharge experiments.
claims.quote: For example, 65°C, CV-overcharge failure experiments occur an order of magnitude faster than RT, CV-overcharge experiments.

claims.claim: The presence of gas bubbles causes significant scattering of ultrasound and significantly reduces signal amplitude, with scattering and attenuation increasing due to bubble resonance as bubbles increase in size, explaining the rapid decline in signal amplitude as batteries approach catastrophic failure.
claims.quote: More overwhelmingly, the presence of gas bubbles causes significant scattering of ultrasound, and the amplitude of the signals is significantly reduced. The scattering and attenuation may increase due to bubble resonance as bubbles increase in size, which can explain the rapid decline in signal amplitude observed as batteries approach catastrophic failure.

claims.claim: After battery failure in high temperature overcharge experiments, the EDX spectrum of the anode surface indicated 57% fluorine, 20% oxygen, and 13% carbon, compared to 15% fluorine, 10% oxygen, and 60% carbon in a new cell, representing a nearly 4x increase in relative fluorine abundance.
claims.quote: Specifically, the spectrum of the new cell electrolyte consists of 60% carbon, 10% oxygen, and 15% fluorine. After the battery is failed in the high temperature overcharge experiments, the EDX spectrum indicates 57% fluorine, 20% oxygen, and 13% carbon on the surface of the anode. After the battery failure, the relative fluorine abundance has increased by nearly 4x.

claims.claim: When a partial CC overcharge was stopped upon reaching the ultrasound warning threshold, battery capacity and coulombic efficiency returned to pre-partial overcharge metrics after resuming normal cycling, suggesting the warning condition represents a recoverable battery state.
claims.quote: After the return to normal cycling, the battery capacity and coulombic efficiency return to pre-partial overcharge metrics (Fig. 8). The results of the partial overcharge experiment suggest the warning scenario is a recoverable battery condition and after a return to normal conditions, its overall capacity retention may not be greatly impacted.

claims.claim: Temperature differences in ultrasound can alter and even reverse the relationships between SoC, amplitude, and time of flight, and temperature often becomes a dominant effect over frequency shifts due to SoC changes.
claims.quote: They observe that temperature often becomes a dominant effect over frequency shifts due to SoC changes. Temperature differences can also alter and even reverse the relationships between SoC, amplitude, and time of flight.

claims.claim: The United Nations Global Technical Regulation on Electric Vehicle Safety requires that a battery system provide either a warning to allow egress or 5 minutes prior to the presence of a hazardous condition that could lead to thermal runaway.
claims.quote: New regulations on EV development in the United Nations Global Technical Regulation on the Electric Vehicle Safety specify that a battery system must provide either a warning to allow egress or 5 min prior to the presence of a hazardous condition that could lead to thermal runaway to be compliant.

claims.claim: Higher percentages of fluorine were observed in CV failure experiment anodes, while CC failure anodes had larger oxygen percentages, caused by rate changes in reaction kinetics induced by high voltage or high current respectively.
