claims.quote: Although post-mortem of all experiments shows increases of fluorine and oxygen in the anode, higher percentages of fluorine are observed in CV failure experiments, while CC failure anodes had larger oxygen percentages. The difference is caused by rate changes in the reaction kinetics induced by high voltage or high current.

claims.claim: In a 280 Ah LiFePO4 multi-cell LIB under thermal abuse, the mass loss during the exhaust reaction stage (from safety vent activation to TR triggering) accounts for over 85% of the total mass loss.
claims.quote: the mass loss during the exhaust reaction stage accounts for over 85 % of the total mass loss

claims.claim: For the 280 Ah multi-cell LIB, 15 seconds of additional disaster development after the critical window is sufficient to make thermal runaway uncontrollable by water mist.
claims.quote: the development speed of side reactions inside the battery was extremely fast, and 15 s is enough to make the evolution of TR uncontrollable

claims.claim: Water mist applied immediately after safety vent activation completely suppressed thermal runaway in the 280 Ah LIB, with lateral battery side temperatures at 122.0°C–149.7°C at the time of suppression.
claims.quote: the TR evolution of the LIB only can be completely cut off when the WM was applied immediately after the activation of the safety vent, at which point the lateral temperature on the side of the battery is 122.0°C–149.7°C

claims.claim: Even when thermal runaway could not be prevented, water mist took away at least 65% of the heat generated by the 280 Ah LIB across different TR development stages.
claims.quote: the WM still exhibits excellent cooling ability, taking away over 65 % of the battery heat accumulation

claims.claim: The presence or absence of ignition of vented gases had little impact on TR evolution of the 280 Ah LIB, with time from safety vent activation to TR differing by only ~1 second (239.7 s without ignition vs. 238.5 s with ignition) and average TR onset temperature differing by only ~0.1°C.
claims.quote: the existence of flames has almost no impact on the evolution process of TR... tSV-TR: Without ignition 239.7, Ignition 238.5... TTR,A: Without ignition 240.7, Ignition 239.9

claims.claim: In the 280 Ah multi-cell LIB, heat accumulation during Stage 3 (TR triggering to end of battery heating) accounts for approximately 49–50% of total heat accumulation, while Stage 2 (safety vent activation to TR triggering) accounts for approximately 23–24%.
claims.quote: The heat accumulated in Stage 2 accounts for approximately 23.6 % and 24.0 % of the total amount... The main heat accumulation occurs in Stage 3, accounting for approximately 49.2 % and 49.9 % of the total amount

claims.claim: In the 280 Ah multi-cell LIB, the internal cells triggered TR nearly simultaneously due to tight arrangement and rapid heat transfer, with no TR propagation phenomenon observed inside the battery.
claims.quote: the battery triggered TR as a whole, and there was no TR propagation phenomenon inside the battery

claims.claim: Water mist extinguished the open flame of the 280 Ah LIB fire within 1 second of application.
claims.quote: from the application of WM to extinguish the open flame is completed within 1 s

claims.claim: The large-capacity 280 Ah LIB exhibited a lower TR control temperature (122.0°C–149.7°C) compared to small-capacity 2.6 Ah 18650 LIB (186.5°C) when water mist was used as the suppression medium.
claims.quote: it is found that the TR of large-capacity LIB evolves rapidly and exhibits a lower TR control temperature... 2.6Ah 18650LIB + WM [35]: TR control temperature 186.5; This work (280Ah LIB + WM): TR control temperature 122.0–149.7

claims.claim: The solid electrolyte interphase film on the anode begins to decompose at 70–120°C under thermal abuse conditions.
claims.quote: The solid electrolyte interphase film on the anode begins to decompose at 70–120°C first

claims.claim: The peak heat release rate for an EV fire is around 6.1 ± 1.7 MW, which is comparable to that of conventional vehicles with a peak heat release rate around 5 MW.
claims.quote: A review of battery fires in electric vehicles by Sun et al. [26] showed that the peak heat release rate for an EV is around 6.1 ± 1.7 MW. This is comparable to statistics for (older) conventional vehicles with a peak heat release rate around 5 MW [27].

claims.claim: Suppressing an EV fire can require between 1,000 L and 10,000 L of water and a quenching time of 15 to 60 minutes, compared to a conventional car fire which is normally extinguished within 5 minutes.
claims.quote: extinguishing EVs required thousands (roughly 1000 L–10,000 L) of liters of water and a quenching time of 15 min–60 min. By comparison, a conventional car fire is normally extinguished within 5 min [29].

claims.claim: External application of water-based fire suppressant to a traction battery pack was insufficient to cool the battery interior, and thermal activity continued with large quantities of gas being released after flames were knocked out.
claims.quote: Cooling inside the battery pack was insignificant if suppressant was applied externally to the pack... as it did not cool the traction battery, thermal activity was not halted, and large quantities of gas continued to be released.

claims.claim: Internal application of water-based fire suppression to a traction battery pack delayed cell-to-cell thermal propagation and prevented thermal runaway from triggering in all cells within the initiating module, using only 13 L of suppressant.
claims.quote: It also delayed how quickly the fire spread from one battery cell, or thermally propagated, to the next cell inside the initiating module. In addition, thermal runaway reactions were not triggered in all cells within this module either... this can be achieved with only 13 L of suppressant as long as there is access to the contents of the traction battery.

claims.claim: Using a threshold of approximately 200°C at the external module surface for triggering thermal runaway propagation, internal suppression systems demonstrated potential to stop thermal runaway from propagating from the initiating module to neighboring modules.
claims.quote: Considering a threshold temperature of ≈ 200°C (temperature at the external surface of the module) for triggering thermal runaway, internal suppression systems a potential to stop thermal runaway propagation as seen from Fig. 16.

claims.claim: The water mist system, with a low flow rate and longer release time of up to several minutes, provided a stronger and more uniform cooling effect throughout the traction battery compared to the water spray system, which released agent in approximately 30 seconds.
claims.quote: A reason for this could be the difference in release time of the agents. Specifically, 30 s for the water spray system and up to several minutes for the water mist systems... the water mist system provided a stronger cooling effect throughout the traction battery.

claims.claim: After external flames are knocked out by fire suppression, large quantities of combustible gas may continue to be released inside the battery pack, creating a risk of gas explosion from sparks or other ignition sources in enclosed spaces.
claims.quote: activity proceeded inside the pack and a large amount of gas was produced. If allowed to accumulate in an enclosed space, there is a risk for a gas explosion due to sparks from the battery or other potential ignition sources.

claims.claim: Voltages greater than 30 V AC or 60 V DC are classified as high voltages within the vehicle industry and are generally considered lethal, requiring more stringent safety procedures.
claims.quote: Voltages greater than 30 V of alternating current (AC) or 60 V DC are classified as high voltages within the vehicle industry [8, 9]. Such voltage levels are generally considered as being lethal and require more stringent safety procedures to be followed [10].

claims.claim: A damaged EV containing a burned or damaged LIB should be stored at least 15 m from structures, combustible materials, or other vehicles until the battery is discharged, as recommended by NFPA.
claims.quote: NFPA's Hybrid and electric vehicle emergency field guide [37]... recommends that a vehicle containing a burned or damaged LIB is stored at least 15 m from structures, combustible materials or other vehicles until the battery is discharged [37].

claims.claim: Lower state of charge in lithium-ion batteries results in significantly less severe burning behavior, which is why LIBs shipped under UN regulations are required to have less than 30% charge.
claims.quote: Lower charge levels mean a more 'friendly' fire behavior, with a significantly less severe burning behavior. This is one of the reasons why LIBs shipped according to UN regulations have less than 30% charge.

claims.claim: When battery SOC is higher than 60%, the battery will experience violent thermal runaway phenomena such as explosions and spouting fire after three-point bending tests.
claims.quote: When the SOC is higher than 60 %, the battery will experience violent thermal runaway phenomena such as explosions and spouting fire after bending tests.

claims.claim: The maximum load-bearing capacity of the battery increases with increasing SOC, with the maximum load at 100% SOC being 16.67% higher than at 0% SOC.
claims.quote: The maximum load of the battery increases with the increase in SOC and the maximum load of the lithium-ion battery at 100 % SOC is 16.67 % higher than that of the lithium-ion battery at 0 % SOC.

claims.claim: The displacement corresponding to the internal short circuit decreases with increasing SOC, indicating that high SOC batteries are more prone to internal short circuits under external force.
claims.quote: the higher the SOC, the smaller the displacement corresponding to the short circuit, indicating that high SOC batteries are more prone to internal short circuits when subjected to external force and displacement.

claims.claim: The average temperature rise rate after internal short circuit is positively correlated with SOC, with rates of 0.54, 2.10, 2.45, 21.92, 37.39, and 52.85 °C/s for 0%, 20%, 40%, 60%, 80%, and 100% SOC respectively.
claims.quote: The average temperature rise rates are 0.54 ◦C/s, 2.10 ◦C/s, 2.45 ◦C/s, 21.92 ◦C/s, 37.39 ◦C/s and 52.85 ◦C/s.

claims.claim: The mass loss rate of 100% SOC batteries after bending test is as high as 30.58%, while at 0% SOC it is only 5.18%, demonstrating that higher SOC significantly increases mass loss rate.
claims.quote: The higher the battery SOC, the greater the mass loss rate and the mass loss rate of a 100 % SOC battery is as high as 30.58 %.

claims.claim: Under the same SOC conditions (40% SOC), electrode thickness has almost no effect on the mass loss rate of the battery, with Cell-A and Cell-B showing mass loss rates of 8.82% and 8.17% respectively.
claims.quote: Under the same conditions of SOC (40 % SOC), the lithium-ion battery triggers an internal short circuit, the electrode thickness has almost no effect on the mass loss rate of the battery.

claims.claim: After internal short circuit, batteries with thicker electrodes exhibit a larger number of broken particles in the cathode material and higher surface roughness on broken particles compared to batteries with thinner electrodes.
claims.quote: After the internal short circuit occurs, due to the breakage of secondary particles, the number of broken positive electrode material particles in the thick electrode is higher than that in the thin electrode and the roughness of the thick electrode is higher than that of the thin electrode.

claims.claim: In thick electrode batteries, during a short circuit, a large amount of Li+ preferentially intercalates into material particles in the upper layer of the cathode, causing volume expansion of the lithiated region while the non-lithiated region remains unchanged, generating stress that leads to secondary particle breakage.
claims.quote: In batteries with thicker electrodes, more ohmic heat is generated... a large amount of Li+ preferentially embeds into the material particles in the upper layer of the positive electrode. The volume of the lithiated area expands, while the volume of the non-lithiated area remains unchanged, causing stress on the original agglomerate and leading to the rupture of secondary particles.

claims.claim: After internal short circuit, the intensity of internal electrochemical reactions in NCM far exceeds that of LFP, as evidenced by significant structural changes in both the separator and cathode material of NCM, while only the separator structure is significantly affected in LFP.
claims.quote: the intensity of the internal electrochemical reactions in NCM far exceeds that of LFP. Comparing the SEM images of the electrode changes in LFP and NCM, it is observed that the changes in the cathode material of LFP are relatively small, with only the separator structure being affected. In contrast, not only does the separator structure change in NCM, but the positive electrode material structure also undergoes significant changes.

claims.claim: Temperature rise lags behind voltage drop after internal short circuit, with lag times of 9 s for Cell-A (NCM thick electrode) and 24 s for Cell-B (NCM thin electrode), making voltage the recommended parameter for initial detection of internal short circuits.
claims.quote: When an internal short circuit occurs, both Cell-A and Cell-B experience significant changes in temperature rise and voltage drop, with temperature changes lagging behind the voltage drop and the lag times are 9 s and 24 s, respectively... it is recommended to use voltage as the judgment method for the initial detection of internal short circuits in batteries.

claims.claim: The separator pores irreversibly close due to melting caused by high internal temperature after internal short circuit, and this closure helps improve the battery's tolerance to abusive conditions.
claims.quote: Due to the internal short circuit, the high temperature causes the separator to melt and the pores to close. The closure of the separator helps to improve the battery's tolerance to abusive conditions.

claims.claim: LFP batteries exhibit better thermal stability than NCM batteries after internal short circuit because the strong covalent P–O bond in the tetrahedral (PO4)3− anions inhibits oxygen loss, reducing ignitable electrolyte mass during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The strong covalent P–O bond in the tetrahedral (PO4)3− anions of the LFP battery cathode can inhibit oxygen loss. A smaller amount of oxygen will reduce the ignitable electrolyte mass during thermal runaway, thereby reducing the heat generated by the battery.

claims.claim: The Young's modulus of graphite increases threefold when lithiated to LiC6 and is linearly related to lithium concentration, contributing to increased battery stiffness and load-bearing capacity at higher SOC.
claims.quote: Reference [33] used density functional theory to reveal that the Young's modulus of graphite increases threefold when lithiated to LiC6 and the Young's modulus is linearly related to the lithium concentration.

claims.claim: At 80°C, the main component of lithium salt LiPF6 decomposes into PF5- and LiF, which deposit on the SEI film surface to form a rock salt layer.
claims.quote: At around 80 ◦C, the main component of the lithium salt, LiPF6, decomposes into PF₅- and LiF, which deposit on the SEI film surface to form a rock salt layer.

claims.claim: Cell-A (NCM, thick electrode, 2600 mAh) exhibits a maximum temperature of 129.07°C and average temperature rise rate of 2.45°C/s at 40% SOC, while Cell-C (LFP, 1530 mAh) exhibits 123.48°C and 2.10°C/s under the same conditions.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature and average temperature rise rate for Cell-A were 129.07 ◦C and 2.45 ◦C/s, while for Cell-C, they were 123.48 ◦C and 2.10 ◦C/s.

claims.claim: The PTC effect is triggered at temperatures above 80 °C, causing PVDF volume expansion that disrupts electron flow and leads to a dramatic increase in cathode resistance, thereby preventing thermal runaway.
claims.quote: At high temperatures (>80 °C), the PTC effect was triggered in the protective layer by PVDF volume expansion, which effectively disrupted electron flow, leading to a dramatic increase in cathode resistance, thus preventing thermal runaway from occurring.

claims.claim: PVDF expands by approximately 50% at 80 °C, which is sufficient to sever or severely interrupt conductive paths in the composite and activate the PTC effect.
claims.quote: PVDF expands by about 50% at 80 °C, which is sufficient to sever or severely interrupt conductive paths in composite and activate the PTC effect.

claims.claim: The impedance of the cell with the PTC layer cathode increases by approximately 450% by 120 °C relative to normal operating cell impedance, whereas the normal cell impedance decreases above 60 °C due to lower charge transfer resistivity and ion diffusion resistivity.
claims.quote: the impedance of the cell with PTC layer cathode increases rapidly above 80 °C with a ~450% increase by 120 °C, indicating the destruction of conduction pathways between Al foil and the cathode active materials layer... However, for the normal cell, the impedance decreases due to the lower charge transfer resistivity and ion diffusion resistivity as the temperature increase above 60 °C.

claims.claim: During overcharge testing to 2Vmax at 25 °C, the cell temperature reached approximately 120 °C for the normal cell but only approximately 70 °C for the cell with the PTC safety layer.
claims.quote: the cells' temperatures increased to ~120 °C for the normal cell but only ~70 °C for the cell with PTC safety layer.

claims.claim: Incorporating a 2–3 μm PTC layer reduces the volume and weight energy density of the cell by only 1.8%–2.0% and 1.4%–1.6%, respectively, compared with cells without the PTC layer.
claims.quote: With 2−3 μm PTC layer, the volume and weight energy density of cell with the PTC layer will only be reduced 1.8%−2.0% and 1.4%−1.6%, respectively, compared with the cell without PTC layer.

claims.claim: The capacity retention of cells with the PTC layer remains at 90% after 1350 cycles with no fading acceleration observed compared to cells without the PTC layer.
claims.quote: the long-term cycle performance was also kept at the same level. The capacity retention still remains at 90% after 1350 cycles. There is no fading acceleration is observed with the addition of PTC layer.

claims.claim: External pressure release vents and PTC resistors on cell casings, which are widely used in commercial LIBs, cannot fully prevent the cell from internal thermal runaway events.
claims.quote: external pressure release vents and positive temperature coefficient (PTC) resistors on cell casings, which are widely used in commercial LIBs, but these cannot fully prevent the cell from internal thermal runaway events.

claims.claim: In 280 Ah LFP batteries, the stress-based early warning method identifies the transition point from exponential to linear stress growth and provides thermal runaway prediction approximately 2000 s in advance at 50% SOC.
claims.quote: By monitoring transition point of stress variation, thermal runaway can be predicted up to approximately 2000 s in advance.

claims.claim: For 280 Ah LFP batteries, stress change rates at the transition point from exponential to linear growth are both above 0.47 kPa/s across all tested SOC conditions, and 0.45 kPa/s is recommended as the practical early warning threshold.
claims.quote: the repeated experiments conducted for each working condition indicate that the stress change rates corresponding to the transition points of these two stages are both higher than 0.47 kPa/s. Therefore, considering the safety margin, 0.45 kPa/s can be regarded as the starting value for the warning.

claims.claim: For a 100% SOC 280 Ah LFP battery, the maximum temperature during thermal runaway (TTR,max) reaches 427.3 °C, which exceeds that of the 50% SOC battery (317.1 °C) by nearly 110 °C.
claims.quote: during thermal runaway, the maximum temperature (TTR, max) reaches 427.3 ℃ for the 100 % SOC battery, compared to only 317.1 ℃ for the 50 % SOC battery. Notably, the maximum temperature for the 100 % SOC battery exceeds that for the 50 % SOC battery by nearly 110 ℃.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway trigger time for the 100% SOC 280 Ah LFP battery is nearly 1200 s earlier than for the 50% SOC battery under identical 500 W overheating conditions.
claims.quote: the thermal runaway trigger time for the 100 % SOC is nearly 1200 s earlier than that for the 50 % SOC.

claims.claim: For the 0% SOC 280 Ah LFP battery under 500 W heating, thermal runaway does not occur even as the front surface temperature approaches 400 °C, demonstrating that lower SOC reduces thermal runaway risk.
claims.quote: for the 0 % SOC battery, the temperature rise rate remains slow until Tf approaches 400 ℃, suggesting that thermal runaway does not occur.

claims.claim: Traditional voltage drop and temperature surge monitoring methods provide only 0–20 s of warning before thermal runaway in 280 Ah LFP batteries, whereas safety valve opening provides approximately 955–1153 s of advance warning depending on SOC.
claims.quote: traditional methods, such as monitoring voltage drop or temperature surge, are only effective within 20 s before thermal runaway occurs, posing a high risk. Detection of gas release can provide an early warning of thermal runaway nearly 1000 s in advance.

claims.claim: During overheating of 280 Ah LFP batteries, the surface stress distribution when the safety valve opens is weaker at the edges and stronger in the middle, attributed to edge constraints reducing peripheral stress while concentrating stress centrally.
claims.quote: the stress distribution on the battery surface is weaker around the edges and stronger in the middle. This phenomenon may be attributed to the constraint at the battery's edges, which reduce stress at the periphery while increasing stress in the central region.

claims.claim: The peak mass loss rate during thermal runaway for the 100% SOC 280 Ah LFP battery (7.24 g/s) is significantly higher than for the 50% SOC battery (2.72 g/s), attributed to faster internal chemical reactions at higher SOC.
claims.quote: the mass loss rate curve for the 50 % SOC battery peaks at 2.72 g/s during thermal runaway, significantly lower than the peak value of 7.24 g/s observed for the 100 % SOC battery.

claims.claim: In 280 Ah LFP batteries, a temperature drop occurs when the safety valve opens due to the Joule-Thomson effect, as high-pressure gas expands through the valve and performs work on the external environment.
claims.quote: the temperature of the batteries at different SOCs decreases to some extent when the safety valve opens, which is attributed to the Joule-Thomson effect. As the gas expands through the valve to perform work on the external environment, the overall temperature decreases.

claims.claim: Gas detection from battery safety valve opening is not a universally reliable early warning method because in some battery types, thermal runaway can occur approximately 10 s after the safety valve opens.
claims.quote: not all batteries exhibit such a prolonged interval between the opening of the safety valve and thermal runaway. In the experiment conducted by Chuang et al. (2024), who used LiNi0.5Co0.2Mn0.3O2/Graphite commercialized lithium-ion batteries to study the characteristics of thermal runaway expansion force, thermal runaway occurred approximately 10 s after the safety valve opened.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway of 280 Ah LFP batteries, the stress variation can be divided into four stages: exponential growth, linear growth, abrupt drop (coinciding with safety valve opening), and rebound (coinciding with thermal runaway onset).
claims.quote: Real-time monitoring of surface stress using thin-film pressure sensors reveals that stress variation during thermal runaway can be divided into four distinct stages: exponential growth, linear growth, sudden drop, and rebound.

claims.claim: In 280 Ah LFP batteries at 50% SOC, the exponential stress growth phase (Stage 1) occurs when battery temperature is below 121.0 °C and is driven by electrolyte evaporation and SEI decomposition.
claims.quote: In Stage 1, when the battery temperature remains below 121.0 ℃, the temperature increases steadily, accompanied by a rise in internal pressure due to electrolyte evaporation and SEI decomposition. This results in an exponential increase in stress.

claims.claim: During high-rate discharge of a 20 Ah lithium-ion phosphate battery, the temperature difference between the battery interior and exterior can reach 20 °C, creating blind spots in thermal monitoring.
claims.quote: Grandjean et al. (2017) found that, during high-rate discharge of a 20 Ah lithium-ion phosphate battery, the temperature difference between the battery's interior and exterior could reach 20 ℃.

claims.claim: For type 21700 NMC cylindrical cells with 100% SOC, the first sharp rise in temperature exhibited a rate greater than 15°C/s, with the shortest time difference between onset of temperature rise and ignition being 3.3 s.
claims.quote: the first sharp rise in temperature for 100% SOC cells exhibited a rate of temperature rise greater than 15°C/s... The duration of combustion was found to be greater than 10 s for 100% SOC cells, with the shortest time difference between the onset of temperature rise and ignition being 3.3 s, which is very short for any intervention to reduce cell temperature to prevent TR.

claims.claim: The average temperature at the first sharp rise in temperature across all six tests (100% and 30% SOC) was 202.0°C, which is identified as a probable critical point at which a large amount of heat is generated by internal short circuit due to separator melting.
claims.quote: An average of temperature at the first sharp rise in temperature of Tests 1–6 is 202.0°C, which is probably a critical point at which a large amount of heat is generated by internal short circuit due to separator melting.

claims.claim: For type 21700 NMC cells with 30% SOC, no violent jet fires were observed, the top cover was not blown away by ejected gases, and the combustion duration was shorter compared to 100% SOC cells, indicating a much less dynamic combustion process.
claims.quote: No violent jet fires were observed, which was significantly different from the tests for 100% SOC cells... The top cover of the cell was not blown away by the ejected gases, indicating a much less dynamic combustion process for 30% SOC cells when compared to the 100% SOC counterparts.

claims.claim: The amount of CO2 within ejected gas streams decreases with increasing SOC, indicating that vent gas mixtures produced by higher SOC cells have a wider flammability range and are more easily ignited.
claims.quote: the amount of CO2 within the ejected gas streams has been shown to decrease with increasing SOC indicating that vent gas mixtures produced by higher SOC cells have a wider flammability range and are more easily ignited.

claims.claim: The estimated peak HRRs for 100% SOC type 21700 NMC cells were 2.88±0.68 kW, 3.08±0.73 kW, and 2.95±0.69 kW, which are less than the value of 3.75±0.86 kW reported for type 18650 LIBs with a similar cathode composition.
claims.quote: The estimated peak HRRs for tests 1, 2 and 3 are 2.88±0.68 kW, 3.08±0.73 kW, and 2.95±0.69 kW, respectively. These estimates are larger than the previously reported peak HRRs of 1.1 kW and 1.3 kW, but less than the value of 3.75±0.86 kW for 18650 LIBs with a similar cathode.

claims.claim: The oxygen consumption method used to calculate HRR is prone to overestimation due to formation of carbon monoxide and soot particles, and to underestimation due to oxygen release from decomposition of cathode materials.
claims.quote: All HRRs reported in the literature were calculated based on the oxygen consumption method, which is prone to both overestimation (due to the formation of carbon monoxide and soot particles) and underestimation (due to oxygen release from the decomposition of the cathode materials).

claims.claim: The absence of blue spark colouration during early-stage thermal runaway of type 21700 cells implies no copper current collector degradation, indicating the internal cell temperature was between the melting point of aluminium (660.32°C) and the melting point of copper (1084.62°C) during that period.
claims.quote: No blue colouration was observed during the early stage of TR, it is inferred that no degradation of the copper current collector was taking place. From this it can be reasoned that the temperature inside the decomposing cell was between the melting point of aluminium (660.32°C) and the melting point of copper (1084.62°C) during this period.

claims.claim: The crack pressure required for a lithium-ion battery relief valve to open in Lhasa (64.3 kPa) is 36.5 kPa less than that in Hefei (100.8 kPa), meaning LIBs crack more easily and faster under low environmental pressure.
claims.quote: the pressure required for the LIB to crack in Lhasa is 36.5 kPa less than that in Hefei. It can be concluded that LIB will crack more easily and faster under low environmental pressure.

claims.claim: The burning rate of common fuels is proportional to the 1.3 power of environmental pressure, meaning that as environmental pressure decreases, the burning rate slows.
claims.quote: 𝑚̇ ′′~𝑃𝛼 (≈1.3) ... The burning rate is proportional to the 1.3 power of the environmental pressure, which means that as the environmental pressure decreases, the burning rate slows.

claims.claim: For LiFePO4 batteries, the total heat release (THR) values at 100.8 kPa (Hefei) are 90 kJ, 171 kJ, and 225 kJ for 0%, 50%, and 100% SOC, respectively, while at 64.3 kPa (Lhasa) they are 70 kJ, 133 kJ, and 168 kJ.
claims.quote: The calculated average THR values of LiFePO4 batteries in Hefei (100.8 kPa) are 90 kJ, 171 kJ, and 225 kJ for 0%, 50%, and 100% SOC, and those in Lhasa (64.3 kPa) were 70 kJ, 133 kJ, and 168 kJ, respectively.

claims.claim: Environmental pressure has little impact on total mass loss (TML) of LIBs during combustion; the maximum TML difference between 100.8 kPa and 64.3 kPa is 1.1 g for 100% SOC LiFePO4 battery and 1.7 g for 100% SOC LiCoO2 battery.
claims.quote: The environmental pressure has little impact on the mass loss. The maximum TML difference between two pressures is 1.1g for 100% SOC LiFePO4 battery and 1.7g for 100% SOC LiCoO2 battery.

claims.claim: The unit growth rate of combustion heat per 1% increase in SOC is higher at high environmental pressure than at low environmental pressure, and this difference in unit growth rate increases with SOC.
claims.quote: The unit growth rate in combustion heat between the two pressures also increases with the SOC... batteries at high environmental pressure have a greater unit growth rate of combustion heat for every 1% increase in SOC.

claims.claim: For 100% SOC LiCoO2 battery, the THR is less than that of the 50% SOC LiCoO2 battery because the intense ejection at 100% SOC causes a large amount of combustibles to not be effectively combusted, decreasing the heat release rate.
claims.quote: The difference is that the THR of the 100% SOC LiCoO2 battery is less than that of 50% SOC battery. The main reason for this result is that the ejection behavior of the LiCoO2 battery at 100% SOC is more intense, and a large amount of combustibles are not effectively combusted, thus causing a decrease in the heat release rate.

claims.claim: In the extreme case where all cathode material decomposes, the LiFePO4 (SONY 18650) battery generates approximately 0.52 g of oxygen while the LiCoO2 (SAMSUNG 18650) battery generates approximately 2.94 g of oxygen.
claims.quote: the amount of oxygen generated is 0.52 g and 2.94 g for LiFePO4 and LiCoO2 battery, respectively.

claims.claim: SEI layer decomposition occurs at 90-120°C during thermal runaway in lithium-ion batteries.
claims.quote: （a) Solid electrolyte interface (SEI) layer decomposition: at 90-120℃.

claims.claim: Reaction of intercalated lithium ions with electrolyte occurs above 120°C during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: （b) Reaction of intercalated lithium ions with electrolyte: >120℃.

claims.claim: Electrolyte decomposition occurs above 200°C during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: （d) Electrolyte decomposition: >200℃.

claims.claim: In a LiCoO2/C battery oven exposure test at 150°C, SEI layer decomposition started at about 70°C and the anode/electrolyte reaction began at about 120°C.
claims.quote: The analysis results manifested that the SEI layer decomposition started at about 70℃ and the anode/electrolyte reaction began at about 120℃.

claims.claim: The anode/electrolyte reaction generates enough heat to further trigger electrolyte and cathode decomposition reactions, which release heat and gas and can cause explosion.
claims.quote: It was pointed out that the anode/electrolyte reaction churn out enough heat to further trigger the electrolyte and cathode decomposition reactions which gave off amounts of heat and gas and even caused an explosion.

claims.claim: ISC-induced thermal runaway can be mainly ascribed to separator melt-down when low-melting-point separators are used, and to electrode decomposition when high-melting-point separators are employed.
claims.quote: the experimental results indicated that the ISC-induced thermal runaway can be mainly ascribed to separator melt-down when low-melting-point separators used and to high-melting-point electrodes decomposition when separators employed.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway due to ISC is dependent on localized heating energy of the ISC spot and its duration, separator shrinkage and melting point and propagation, and overall cell temperature rise.
claims.quote: indicated that the thermal runaway was dependent on: （a) localized heating energy of the ISC spot and its duration. （b) separator shrinkage, melting point and propagation. （c) overall cell temperature rise.

claims.claim: In nail penetration tests, temperature difference within an individual cell was as high as 791.8°C, and heat transfer via electrode connectors was only 1/10 of that via the shell.
claims.quote: Temperature difference within an individual cell was as high as 791.8℃; (c) Heat transfer via electrode connectors was only 1/10 of that via the shell

claims.claim: Thermal runaway of LIBs caused by nail penetration was primarily determined by the total Joule heat generated by the ISC current.
claims.quote: Yamauchi et al. [46] found that thermal runaway of LIBs caused by nail penetration was primarily determined by the total Joule heat generated by the ISC current.

claims.claim: Direct-contact liquids achieve 1.5 to 3 times higher cooling performance than air cooling, while indirect-contact liquids achieve more than 3 times better performance than air cooling only at the expense of efficiency droop.
claims.quote: direct-contact liquids have much higher heat transfer rate and can achieve 1.5 to 3 times higher cooling performance than the air cooling. Indirect-contact liquids, usually water or water/glycol solutions, is able to achieve more than 3 times better performance than the air cooling only at the expense of efficiency droop.

claims.claim: Air cooling was concluded to be only suitable for parallel HEVs, while EVs and series HEVs required a better heat transfer medium.
claims.quote: Pesaran et al. [82] concluded that the air cool might be only suitable for the use in parallel HEVs while EVs and series HEVs required a better heat transfer medium.

claims.claim: A reciprocating air flow thermal management method was experimentally proved capable of reducing temperature difference by 4°C (72%) in 120 seconds.
claims.quote: Mahamud and Park [83] introduced a reciprocating air flow thermal management method that was experimentally proved capable of reducing the temperature difference by 4℃ (72%) in 120s.

claims.claim: A DC voltage of 19V was sufficient for 18650 battery cells to maintain an electrical arc during current interruption, which is well lower than the upper limit of 48V permitted by the National Electrical Code.
claims.quote: a DC voltage of 19V was sufficient for 18650 battery cells to maintain an electrical arc, which was well lower than the upper limit of 48V permitted by the National Electrical Code.

claims.claim: Placement of phase change composite (PCC) material between cells in a LIB module decreased the maximum temperature experienced by surrounding cells by 60°C, indicating effective hindrance of thermal runaway propagation.
claims.quote: experimental studies showed that the maximum temperature experienced by the surrounding cells was decreased by 60℃, which indicated an effective hindrance of thermal runaway propagation within the pack.

claims.claim: Temperature is usually sampled on the cell surface, and there is a significant discrepancy between the core and surface when thermal runaway events occur, which impairs early-stage thermal runaway detection.
claims.quote: temperature is usually sampled on the cell surface, and there would be a significant discrepancy between the core and surface when thermal runaway events occur. This inevitably impairs the prediction ability for early-stage thermal runaway detection.

claims.claim: Overcharge of the negative electrode causing lithium deposition greatly decreases the cycle life of LIBs and poses serious safety hazards.
claims.quote: Arora et al. [48] revealed that overcharge of the negative electrode that causes lithium deposition would greatly decrease the cycle life of LIBs and pose serious safety hazard

claims.claim: Higher current rates and increased initial temperatures markedly increase thermal hazards in lithium-ion batteries under adiabatic cycling conditions.
claims.quote: Wang et al. [62] conducted battery cycling experiments in an adiabatic environment and found out that higher current rates and increased initial temperatures would markedly increase thermal hazards.

claims.claim: In a nail penetration-induced thermal runaway test, the penetration-induced test offered a lower onset temperature compared with an ARC-based oven-heat-induced thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Compared with an ARC-based oven-heat-induced thermal runaway, a penetration-induced test offered a lower onset temperature

claims.claim: When battery temperature exceeds approximately 90°C, decomposition of the SEI film is triggered, which is defined as the beginning of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: when the temperature is near 90 oC or even higher, the decomposition of SEI film can be triggered, which is defined as the beginning of thermal runaway.

claims.claim: When battery temperature exceeds 40°C, the lifespan of lithium ion battery may shorten due to speeding side reactions.
claims.quote: As the temperature is higher than 40 oC, the lifespan of lithium ion battery may shorten due to the speeding side reactions

claims.claim: In thermal runaway, heat generation caused by internal short circuit is only 2%, while chemical reactions account for 98% of the total heat generation.
claims.quote: In thermal runaway process, heat generation caused by ISC is only 2%, and chemical reactions are 98%, including the decomposition of SEI layer, decomposition of electrolyte, reaction of graphite with binder decomposition of cathode materials etc.

claims.claim: The PE/PP separator melts at approximately 130°C, causing internal short circuit and leading to more severe thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The polyethylene (PE)/polypropylene (PP) separator will melt as the temperature higher than ~130 oC, causing ISC and leading to more serious abuse condition.

claims.claim: Decomposition of cathode materials in lithium ion batteries may occur at temperatures above approximately 180°C, accompanied by high heat generation and significant oxygen release.
claims.quote: as the temperature is higher than ~180 oC, decomposition of cathode in lithium ion battery may occur, accompanied with highly heat generation and significant oxygen release.

claims.claim: The largest proportion of heat generation during thermal runaway is caused by rapid oxidation-reduction reaction between the cathode and anode (Ca+An), accounting for about 48% of total thermal release.
claims.quote: The largest proportion of heat generation is caused by rapid oxidation-reduction reaction between the cathode and anode (Ca+An), about 48%, while the heat generation of other chemical reactions in anode, cathode and electrolyte is much smaller.

claims.claim: The onset temperature of phase transition of Ni-rich NMC materials is approximately 100°C due to thermally induced reduction of Ni ions.
claims.quote: The onset temperature of phase transition of Ni-rich NMC materials is quite low (about 100 oC) due to the existing thermally induced reduction of Ni ions

claims.claim: When Ni content in NMC cathode rises to 0.95, the phase transition temperature decreases to 180°C, indicating very low thermal stability.
claims.quote: As the Ni content rise up to 0.95, the phase transition temperature decrease to 180 oC, a quit low thermal stability.

claims.claim: Mini-channel cooling cannot stop thermal runaway in a single cell even at flow rates up to 10 L/min, but can prevent thermal runaway propagation between cells.
claims.quote: the mini-channel cannot stop the thermal runway in single cell even as the flow rate is up to 10 L/min, but can prevent thermal runaway propagation between cells.

claims.claim: Heat pipe-based battery thermal management can only prevent thermal runaway propagation between two adjacent batteries but cannot prevent thermal runaway in a single battery.
claims.quote: the heat pipe can only prevent the thermal runaway propagation between two adjacent batteries, but cannot prevent the thermal runaway in single battery.

claims.claim: To prevent thermal runaway propagation between cells using phase change materials, the latent heat capacity of the PCM must reach up to 1125 kJ/kg.
claims.quote: In order to achieve better thermal performance, the latent heat capacity of phase change materials should up to 1125 kJ/kg, a really high enthalpy.

claims.claim: At temperatures below 0°C, slow electrochemical kinetics can induce lithium dendrites, volume expansion, and delamination of active materials, leading to swelling failure inside lithium ion batteries.
claims.quote: the slow electrochemical kinetics at low temperature can induce Li dendrites, volume expansion and delamination of active materials, leading to swelling failure inside lithium ion battery

claims.claim: Overdischarge may result in dissolution of copper current collector, and subsequent copper deposition can form copper dendrites, leading to internal short circuit inside the battery.
claims.quote: the overdischarge may result in the dissolution of copper, and the deposition of the copper can form a copper dendrites, leading to ISC inside the battery.

claims.claim: During external short circuit, as temperature reaches 77°C to 121°C, cell rupture, venting, and electrolyte leakage may be triggered.
claims.quote: As the temperature reached up to a highly level (77 oC ~121 oC), the cell rupture, venting and electrolyte leakage may be triggered.

claims.claim: Oxygen release from NMC cathode materials occurs when approximately 81% of Li-ions are removed during charging, caused by the formation of surface spinel and/or rock-salt phases.
claims.quote: they find that the oxygen release will occur as the about 81% of Li-ions remove from layered cathode materials, which is caused by the formation of surface spinel and/or rock-salt phases as the state of charge reaches 81% in charging process.

claims.claim: The thermal stability of NMC cathode materials decreases with increasing Ni content.
claims.quote: The thermal stability of NMC cathode materials decreases with the increasing Ni content.

claims.claim: Released oxidative gas from cathode materials transfers to the anode and reacts with reductive LiCx at 274.2°C, accompanied with tremendous heat generation.
claims.quote: the released oxidative gas will transfer to anode and reacts with reductive LiCx at 274.2 oC, accompanied with tremendous heat generation

claims.claim: Inserting a heat pipe in the axis of a cylindrical Li-ion cell for core cooling can achieve 18–20°C temperature reduction.
claims.quote: The results showed that this method could achieve 18-20°C temperature reduction.

claims.claim: Internal heat pipe cooling of cylindrical cells can reduce cell thermal resistance by up to 67.8±1.4%, but leads to a maximum energy density decrease of 6.0% and cell mass increase of 11.7% for 18650 and 32113 cells.
claims.quote: The results showed that the internal cooling strategy could reduce the cell thermal resistance by up to 67.8±1.4%. However, this approach led to a decrease in energy density and an increase in cell mass. Based on Worwood et al.'s results, the maximum energy density decreases and cell mass increase of 18650 and 32113 cells could be 6.0% and 11.7%, respectively.

claims.claim: AC heating with low frequencies and large current can cause complex side reactions and trigger Li plating on the surface of graphite anode, while high frequencies will not induce Li plating.
claims.quote: They reported that AC heating with low frequencies and large current can cause complex side reactions and the Li plating can be triggered on the surface of graphite anode, while high frequencies will not induce Li plating.

claims.claim: In LiCoO2-graphite lithium-ion cells overcharged at 1C rate, the lithium content x in LixCoO2 decreased from x=0.5 at the start of overcharge to x=0.08 at the end of overcharge.
claims.quote: The lithium content (x of LixCoO2) in the cathode was 0.5 at the start of the overcharge, and x = 0.08 at the end of overcharge.

claims.claim: During 1C rate overcharge of prismatic LiCoO2/graphite cells, the cell case temperature reached a maximum of 110°C before dropping, whereas at 2C rate overcharge the cell temperature rise was steep and the cell reached thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The cell case temperature remained low for the first 50 min, and then gradually rose to a maximum temperature of 110 ◦C with the trip of the voltage before depression. When the overcharge was carried out at a 1 C rate, the cell temperature dropped after the peak point of the temperature as shown in Fig. 1. However, in the case of a 2 C rate overcharge, the cell temperature rise was steep and the cell reached a thermal runway.

claims.claim: CO2 gas was produced mainly by oxidation of the electrolyte at the cathode during overcharge, while H2 gas was the main component evolved at the anode.
claims.quote: Carbon monoxide (CO) gas and carbon dioxide (CO2) gas were chiefly generated at the cathode. As for the anode, H2 gas was the main component of the evolved gas, and small amounts of CH4, C2H4, C2H6, CO and CO2 were produced.

claims.claim: The exothermic oxidation reaction of the overcharged cathode with the electrolyte was accelerated at temperatures above 60°C, causing rapid cell temperature increase and large CO2 gas evolution.
claims.quote: Therefore, it turned out that the reaction between the cathode and the electrolyte accelerated rapidly at over 60 ◦C... The oxidation reaction of the electrolyte was accelerated at the temperature above 60 ◦C, causing the cell temperature to increase rapidly thereafter, since the reaction between the overcharged cathode and the electrolyte was exothermic.

claims.claim: Heating tests of overcharged anode samples (stages A–C) enclosed in cylindrical cell cases with EC/EMC electrolyte resulted in thermal runaway and cell rupturing with onset near 130°C, whereas overcharged cathodes tested in the same manner showed no thermal runaway.
claims.quote: While the sample with the anode charged to 4.2 V showed no thermal runaway, the samples for (A)–(C) resulted in thermal runaway and cell rupturing. The starting temperature for thermal runaway for each of the samples (A)–(C) was near 130 ◦C. Although overcharged cathodes were tested in the same manner, no thermal runaway reaction was observed with any cathode.

claims.claim: Overcharged anode samples (stages D and E) in which cell temperature exceeded 90°C during overcharge showed no rupturing during subsequent heating, because the negative electrode surface became inactivated with heat during overcharge.
claims.quote: The overcharged anode samples for (D) and (E) in which cell temperature exceeded 90 ◦C showed no rupturing. This was because the negative electrode surface became inactivated with heat during overcharge.

claims.claim: When the polyethylene separator shutdown temperature of 130–135°C is reached during 1C overcharge, the overcharge current drops steeply and cell temperature falls without rupturing, but at 2C rate the steep temperature rise leads to violent reaction of the overcharged anode and electrolyte causing thermal runaway with cell rupturing.
claims.quote: When the cell internal temperature reaches the shutdown temperature of the polyethylene separator (130–135 ◦C), the overcharge current drops steeply and the cell temperature falls with no rupturing. On the contrary, the steep rise of the cell temperature in the case of a 2 C rate overcharge leads to the violent reaction of the overcharged anode (deposited lithium) and electrolyte.

claims.claim: A large quantity of cobalt was detected on separators of cells overcharged at 60°C or higher temperatures, indicating that cobalt from LiCoO2 dissolved in the electrolyte and deposited on the anode above this temperature threshold.
claims.quote: A large quantity of cobalt was detected on the separators of the cells overcharged at 60 ◦C or higher temperatures. The cathode active material became unstable due to the overcharge dissociated oxygen, and hence, cobalt in LiCoO2 dissolved in the electrolyte and deposited on the anode.

claims.claim: Rapid voltage drop during internal short circuit is caused by direct contact between cathode and anode as a result of separator fracture.
claims.quote: the rapid voltage drop is due to the direct contact between cathode and anode as a result of separator fracture, while the gentle voltage drop is related to the flattening of the separator with the absence of its function to isolate electron transfer

claims.claim: Gentle (gradual) voltage drop during internal short circuit is caused by flattening of the separator without fracture, which eliminates its function of blocking electron transfer.
claims.quote: the gentle voltage drop is related to the flattening of the separator with the absence of its function to isolate electron transfer

claims.claim: Under sphere indentation, the separator was observed to be transparent and free of any crack at the ISC triggering point, serving as direct evidence that ISC can be triggered without separator fracture.
claims.quote: The observations in Fig. 2(c) demonstrate that the separator was transparent but free of any crack. This serves as direct evidence that the ISC can be triggered without separator fracture.

claims.claim: Under cylinder compression, several cracks were observed within the vicinity of the loading area of the separator at the ISC triggering point, indicating mechanical fracture leads to direct cathode-anode contact.
claims.quote: several cracks were observed within the vicinity of the loading area of separator in Fig. 2(f). In this condition, the separator was mechanical damaged (or fractured), which leads to the direct contact between cathode and anode, causing a sharp voltage drop.

claims.claim: The generalized ISC criteria based on separator deformation are defined as volumetric strain threshold εV = −2.11 and equivalent strain threshold εeq = 2.13; ISC is triggered when either threshold is reached or exceeded.
claims.quote: we take the smaller one of the threshold strain value in these two loading scenarios to establish an ISC criterion, i.e., εV = −2.11 [and] εeq = 2.13

claims.claim: The primary loading type the separator mainly bears during both sphere indentation and cylinder compression is compression, as the governing strain is the minimum principal strain whose absolute value is much greater than the maximum and middle principal strains.
claims.quote: The governing strain is minimum principal strain since its absolute value is much greater compared with maximum and middle principal strains, which indicates the primary loading type the separator mainly bears is compression.

claims.claim: Celgard 2500 single-layer polypropylene separator exhibits obvious anisotropic mechanical behavior: MD tension shows maximum tensile strength with minimum failure strain, while TD tension shows the weakest tensile strength.
claims.quote: MD tension shows the maximum tensile strength with the minimum failure strain, while the weakest tensile strength occurs in TD tension, which is mainly attributed to the microstructure and pre-stretching during the manufacturing process of the separator

claims.claim: The voltage drop in ISC experiments occurs after the peak force, implying that electrodes sustain damage earlier than separator failure.
claims.quote: The voltage drop occurs after the peak force which implies the electrodes come to damage earlier than the failure of the separator.

claims.claim: Once ISC is triggered, the generated Ohmic heat inside lithium-ion batteries may activate exothermic (electro)chemical reactions, which may lead to thermal runaway, fires, or explosions.
claims.quote: once an internal short circuit (ISC) is triggered, the generated Ohmic heat inside LIBs may activate the exothermic (electro) chemical reactions, which may lead to thermal runaway, or sometimes even fires or explosions

claims.claim: The proposed ISC criteria (εV = −2.11 and εeq = 2.13) were validated under flat-headed indentation, a loading condition not used in criteria development, confirming their generalizability.
claims.quote: the lines at εV = −2.11 and εeq = 2.13 serve as the ISC triggering threshold values. We observe that the ISC is predicted to appear once the threshold values are reached and no ISC can be seen even if we take one time step back based upon the computation model. Therefore, the proposed criteria also work well upon flat-headed indentation.

claims.claim: LiPF6 can decompose at 60°C to 70°C, but the heat released at this low temperature is low enough to be omitted; however, the product PF5 can accelerate reactions with other components.
claims.quote: LiPF6 has instability of chemistry and thermodynamics. Thus it can decompose at 60~70°C, following (19). Although this reaction happens at low temperature, the heat released is also low that can be omitted. However, the product PF5 can be accelerated to react with other component.

claims.claim: Decomposition of the SEI layer begins at 100°C to 130°C, converting metastable components to stable components and releasing heat.
claims.quote: the decomposition of SEI turns up. The metastable components will turn to be the stable components, and egress of heat, following (10) and (11). The existing researches indicate the decomposition of SEI is the progress that improve the exothermic initial temperature 100~130°C

claims.claim: Once the SEI is decomposed, the negative electrode is exposed to electrolyte and exothermic reactions between intercalated lithium and electrolyte occur; some research indicates these reactions occur at 110°C to 150°C, while other research indicates they occur above 200°C.
claims.quote: Once the SEI is decomposed, the negative electrode is exposed to electrolyte and will directly react with electrolyte, and exothermic reaction between intercalated lithium and electrolyte can occur... Some researches indicate these reactions turn up at 110~150°C, and some others indicate they turn up at above 200°C.

claims.claim: Positive electrode decomposition releasing oxygen occurs at 170°C to 300°C, and the chemical reduction of the positive active material is highly exothermic, with these reactions identified as igniting thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Positive electrode is decomposed and release oxygen at 170~300°C... The existing researches indicate that the chemical reduction of the positive active material is highly exothermic, and just these reactions ignite the thermal runway.

claims.claim: Among several positive electrode materials, the onset temperature for thermal decomposition increases in the order: LiNiO2 (~180°C), LiCoO2 (~180°C), LiMn2O4 (~200°C), LiFePO4 (~220°C), LiNi1/4Co3/8Mn3/8O2 (~270°C), indicating LiNi1/4Co3/8Mn3/8O2 has the greatest thermal stability.
claims.quote: Table II lists the thermal stability of several positive electrodes... LiNiO2: around 180°C, LiCoO2: around 180°C, LiMn2O4: around 200°C, LiFePO4: around 220°C, LiNi1/4Co3/8Mn3/8O2: around 270°C

claims.claim: Above 230°C, PVDF reacts exothermically with the negative electrode releasing heavy heat, but thermal runaway has already been triggered by this point.
claims.quote: Above 230°C, the PVDF can react exothermically with negative electrode, which is according to (18). The existing researches indicate that this reaction can release heavy heat. However, the thermal runway has been triggered at this time.

claims.claim: Under abnormal (thermal runaway) conditions, gases POF3, C2H5F, PF5, and H2 are released in addition to gases (CO2, C2H4, C4H10) that are also present under normal conditions, enabling gas composition to be used to distinguish normal from abnormal battery states.
claims.quote: from Table III, it can be seen that, under normal condition and abnormal condition, some different species of gas are released. Thus, gas difference can be used to diagnose the condition of Li-ion battery

claims.claim: During an external short circuit (ESC) test of an LCO-MCMB coin cell, the internal RTD measured a maximum temperature of 36.4°C while the external RTD measured only 27.6°C, a difference of approximately 9°C.
claims.quote: the internal temperature rose by 12.4°C to a maximum of 36.4°C, but the external temperature only rose to a maximum of 27.6°C

claims.claim: During an overcharge test at 5C rate (4.2 V to 12 V) of an LCO-MCMB coin cell, the internal RTD detected a maximum temperature of 48.4°C while the external RTD measured only 27.9°C, a difference of approximately 20°C.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature recorded by external RTD was 27.9°C, while the maximum internal RTD detected a much higher temperature of 48.4°C during the overcharge process.

claims.claim: The internal RTD achieved 90% of its total temperature rise (t90) 14 times faster than the external RTD during ESC testing, with the internal RTD positioned behind the anode.
claims.quote: the detection ability of internal RTD to measure 90% of the temperature rise (t90) turned out to be 14 times faster as compared to the external RTD

claims.claim: In multimode calorimetry (MMC) testing, the LIB with the embedded RTD and PLA spacer released 1.75 kJ g⁻¹ of heat in the 160–250°C range, compared to 2.41 kJ g⁻¹ for the conventional LIB without RTD, and thermal runaway onset was delayed by 10°C (from 170°C to 180°C).
claims.quote: The heat released from RTD embedded LCO-MCMB full cell was 1.75 kJ g-1. Also, the onset for the conventional cell was 170 °C, whereas in the proposed work there was a delay in the thermal runaway by 10 °C and occurred after 180 °C.

claims.claim: The melting of the PLA spacer during overheating covers the coin cell gasket, preventing atmospheric oxygen from entering the cell and thereby delaying the onset of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: PLA melts and covers the gasket of the coin cell. This prevents the atmospheric oxygen from entering the coin cell to a larger extent delaying the onset of the thermal runaway event.

claims.claim: The readings of the internal and external RTDs begin to diverge at approximately 4.7 V during overcharge, which corresponds to the decomposition voltage of the electrolyte.
claims.quote: The readings of internal and external RTDs begins to differ around 4.7 V, which is typically the decomposition voltage for the electrolyte.

claims.claim: Gas accumulation during thermal runaway can cause deformation of battery construction, which disrupts thermal conduction and invalidates the assumption of uniform temperature distribution even when the Biot number is less than 0.1.
claims.quote: both this study and our previous work indicated that a significant temperature gradient would be present during thermal runaway and appears to be attributed to gas generation during thermal runaway as it has been reported that gas accumulation could result in the deformation of battery construction, which affects the condition of thermal conduction.

claims.claim: At least 346.5 J of laser irradiation is required to induce thermal runaway in NMC811 18650 cylindrical cells at 100% state of charge.
claims.quote: It has been identified that the minimum laser energy required to induce the battery thermal runaway is 346.5 J in 100 % SoC cells.

claims.claim: NMC811 18650 cylindrical cells at 50% state of charge require 495 J of laser energy to induce thermal runaway, compared to 346.5 J at 100% SoC.
claims.quote: At 50 % SoC, the battery cells required 495 J of laser energy.

claims.claim: Laser irradiation of 2.5 seconds duration does not induce thermal runaway in NMC811 18650 cells regardless of state of charge (100% or 50% SoC).
claims.quote: The chamber pressure shows that the LITR do not induce thermal runaway when the irradiation time is 2,5 s independently on the SoC.

claims.claim: Cells at 50% SoC do not enter thermal runaway after 3.5 seconds of laser irradiation, whereas 100% SoC cells irradiated for 3.5 seconds do reach thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The cells with 50 % SoC do not enter thermal runaway after 3.5 s of laser irradiation. Besides, the cases of 100 % SoC with 5 and 3.5 s of laser irradiation have reached 5.53 and 5.85 bar pressure, respectively.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway vented gas ignition is hypothesized to be caused by hot incandescent particles expelled from the jellyroll igniting the flammable vented gases, with the flame front originating on the opposite side of the test chamber from the cell.
claims.quote: the ignition of the gas in the zone with the highest concentration of hot particles may be a reason for proposing the hypothesis that one of the thermal runaway ignition mechanisms corresponds to the hot particles responsible for igniting the flammable vented gases.

claims.claim: Once the transition from the turbulent vent stage to the particle vent stage is achieved, all such cases end in thermal runaway.
claims.quote: This is why all the cases in which the transition between the turbulent to the particle vent is achieved end in the thermal runaway phenomenon.

claims.claim: At 100% SoC with 5 seconds of laser irradiation, the mean maximum air temperature is 400.1°C, whereas at 50% SoC under the same irradiation duration it ranges from 285.5°C to 385.7°C.
claims.quote: at 100 % SoC with a laser irradiation of 5 s, the maximum air temperature has a mean value of 400.1°C, whereas at 50 % SoC, it varies from 285.5°C to 385.7°C.

claims.claim: The voltage drop in laser-irradiation-induced thermal runaway is not directly associated with current interrupt device (CID) decomposition because localized laser heating produces temperature stratification that delays CID decomposition until thermal runaway occurs.
claims.quote: Unlike other abuse methodologies, the voltage drop in these cases is not associated directly with the current interrupt device (CID) decomposition because the local heating of the laser produces a lot of temperature stratification, delaying the CID decomposition until the cell reaches the thermal runaway.

claims.claim: When 5% graphite oxide was added to facilitate exfoliation of kaolinite by high-shear mixer, 95% of obtained nanosheets were distributed between 0.1-0.7 μm with fewer than 5 layers, compared to 74% in the same size range for directly exfoliated kaolinite (0-G/K/P).
claims.quote: the lateral size distribution between 0.1-0.7 μm is 74% for 0-G/K/P and 95% for 5-G/K/P, decreasing gradually

claims.claim: The BET specific surface area of graphite oxide-assisted exfoliated kaolinite (5-G/K/P) was 21.47 m²/g, which is approximately 2.1 times that of directly exfoliated kaolinite (0-G/K/P) at 10.38 m²/g, compared to raw kaolinite at 7.96 m²/g.
claims.quote: The specific surface area of 0-G/K/P and 5-G/K/P is 10.38 and 21.47 m2/g, respectively. The specific surface area of 5-G/K/P is significantly higher than that of 0-G/K/P, which is attributed to the exfoliation of kaolinite layers exposing more surfaces and the formation of nanoscrolls structure.

claims.claim: At a rotational speed of 6000 rpm, the average nanoscroll length decreased to approximately 0.25 μm (about 73% between 0.2–0.3 μm), compared to an average length of 0.49 μm at 4000 rpm, because higher shear creates smaller kaolinite particles that are more difficult to curl into nanoscrolls.
claims.quote: when the rotation speed increases to 6000 rpm, the average length of the nanoscrolls is about 0.25 μm, in which about 73% are between 0.2 to 0.3 μm. The reason is that with the increase of rotational speed, the size of the exfoliated kaolinite particles becomes smaller, making it difficult to curl into nanoscrolls structure.

claims.claim: The theoretical maximum adsorption capacity (Qm) for methylene blue of graphite oxide-assisted exfoliated kaolinite (5-G/K/P) was 250 mg/g, significantly higher than that of directly exfoliated kaolinite (0-G/K/P) at 111 mg/g, and higher than a physical mixture of 5% GO and 95% directly exfoliated kaolinite at 166.65 mg/g, indicating the improvement is not due to simple mixing.
claims.quote: The Qm of 0-G/K/P and 5-G/K/P are 111 and 250 mg/g... The adsorption capacity of 5% GO and 95% directly exfoliated kaolinite is 166.65 mg/g, significantly lower than that of 5-G/K/P (250 mg/g), indicating that the adsorption capacity of 5-G/K/P to MB is not a simple mixed by GO and kaolinite.

claims.claim: For graphite oxide-assisted exfoliated kaolinite (5-G/K/P), the number of kaolinite layers was less than 5, whereas directly exfoliated kaolinite (0-G/K/P) had between 4–46 layers with an average of approximately 14 layers, based on AFM measurements.
claims.quote: the number of layers of 0-G/K/P nanosheets is relatively wide, and between 4-46 layers, the average layers of 0-G/K/P are about 14 layers. For 5-G/K/P, the GO adsorbs the surface of kaolinite nanosheets, thus, the number of kaolinite layers of 5-G/K/P is less than 5 layers according to a single layer thickness of 1.493 nm.

claims.claim: The differences in TRP time and triggering temperature under different trigger modes are obvious in the early stage of TRP but are gradually eliminated in the later TRP stage.
claims.quote: The differences in TRP time and triggering temperature are obvious under different trigger modes in the early stage of TRP, but these differences are gradually eliminated in the later TRP stage.

claims.claim: More than 60% of TR energy is used for battery self-heating during thermal runaway propagation.
claims.quote: the energy ﬂow distribution shows that more than 60% of TR energy is used for battery self-heating and more than 26% of the energy is emitted during cell material explosions.

claims.claim: More than 26% of TR energy is emitted during cell material explosions (ejecta) during thermal runaway propagation.
claims.quote: more than 60% of TR energy is used for battery self-heating and more than 26% of the energy is emitted during cell material explosions.

claims.claim: The nail penetration trigger mode has the shortest TR trigger time at the second level, while the heating trigger has the best comprehensive performance with moderate trigger time, good operability, and low cost.
claims.quote: the nail penetration trigger has the shortest trigger time while the heating trigger has the best comprehensive performance, allowing for good LIB testing operability and incurring low costs.

claims.claim: The overcharge trigger mode requires a special battery test bench and some battery packs have a current protection function, resulting in poor reliability and high test cost.
claims.quote: The overcharge trigger test requires a special battery test bench, and some battery packs have a current protection function, so this trigger mode has poor reliability and a high-test cost.

claims.claim: The maximum internal temperature of cell 1 triggered by nail penetration is the lowest among the three trigger modes because no external energy is introduced in this triggering model.
claims.quote: the internal temperature of cell 1 triggered by nail penetration is the lowest. The reason for this phenomenon is that no external energy is introduced in this triggering model.

claims.claim: For the nail penetration trigger mode in the first TRP, the absence of heat reflux causes the heat flow to the next cell to increase, and the local temperature at the nail penetration point is high, so the TR temperature of the adjacent cell is quickly reached.
claims.quote: For the nail penetration mode in the ﬁrst TRP, owing to the absence of heat reﬂux, the heat ﬂow to cell 2 increases, and the local temperature at the nail penetration point is high, so the TR temperature of cell 2 is quickly reached.

claims.claim: For the heating trigger mode in the first TRP, heat absorbed by the heater decreases the temperature difference between cell 1 and cell 2, reducing heat flux density and resulting in a longer TRP time than the other two trigger methods.
claims.quote: For the heating triggering mode in the ﬁrst TRP, owing to the heat absorbed by the heater, the temperature difference between cell 1 and cell 2 decreases, and the heat ﬂux density decreases. Therefore, the TRP time is longer than that of the other two methods.

claims.claim: The thermophysical parameters of the jelly roll decrease significantly after thermal runaway: density decreases from 2680 to 1990.7 kg/m³, heat capacity from 1100 to 788.47 J/kg/K, in-plane thermal conductivity from 15.30 to 8.18 W/m/K, and through-plane thermal conductivity from 0.84 to 0.35 W/m/K.
claims.quote: Density (kg·m−3): Before TR 2680, After TR 1990.7; Heat capacity (J·kg−1·K−1): Before TR 1100, After TR 788.47; Thermal conductivity in the X direction (W·m−1·K−1): Before TR 15.30, After TR 8.18; Thermal conductivity in the Z direction (W·m−1·K−1): Before TR 0.84, After TR 0.35.

claims.claim: The heat of eruption (H_vent) is calculated as 165 kJ, based on a total mass loss of approximately 210 g during TR, with gas accounting for approximately 44 g and solid particles approximately 166 g.
claims.quote: The ARC test in this study shows that the mass loss is approximately 210 g, so the gas weighs approximately 44 g, and the solid weight is about 166 g. According to Eq. (16), the heat of eruption is 165 kJ, which is indicated as H_vent.

claims.claim: TRP can be inhibited or delayed by improving battery pack structure to reduce the temperature of ejecta or to expel ejecta to prevent explosions, since ejecta heat accounts for a considerable proportion of total TR energy.
claims.quote: TRP can be inhibited or delayed by improving the structure of the battery pack to reduce the temperature of the eruption or expel the ejecta to prevent explosions.

claims.claim: As TRP progresses, the maximum temperature of cells under different triggering modes converges and fluctuates around 900 °C.
claims.quote: As TRP progressed, the maximum temperature of cells under different triggering modes began to converge, ﬂuctuating around 900 °C.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature of TR caused by overcharge at SOC=165% is 175 °C higher than the maximum temperature measured by ARC at SOC=100% for the same soft package cell chemistry.
claims.quote: The experimental results showed that the maximum temperature of the TR experiment is 899 °C (SOC = 165%), while the maximum temperature of the ARC experiment is 724 °C (SOC = 100%). We can see that the difference between them is 175 °C.

claims.claim: Only about 1% of TR energy is convected and radiated through the shell of cells undergoing thermal runaway propagation.
claims.quote: Only about 1% of the TR energy is convected and radiated through the shell of the cells undergoing TR.

claims.claim: More than 7% of TR heat is injected into the next cell to trigger TR propagation, and about 2% of TR heat is returned to the previous cell.
claims.quote: more than 7% of TR heat is injected into the next cell to cause the TR of the next cell to maintain TRP, and about 2% of TR heat is returned to the previous cell.

claims.claim: The active layer affords only about 5% of total force in tensile loading of electrodes, and electrode failure strain is consistent with that of current collectors, showing that failure of the current collector triggers electrode failure under both quasi-static and dynamic conditions.
claims.quote: The comparison of experimental results shows that the active layer only affords about 5% of total force, and electrodes exhibit a similar elastoplastic behavior just like the metal foils. Moreover, the failure strain of electrodes is consistent with that of current collectors, showing that the failure of current collector happens to trigger the electrode failure at both quasi-static and dynamic condition.

claims.claim: The cathode has a tensile failure strain of 0.01 and yield strength of 140 MPa, while the anode has a tensile failure strain of 0.02 and yield strength of 220 MPa, creating an incompatibility in strength and stiffness that could be the reason for battery internal failure.
claims.quote: the cathode only has failure strain of 0.01 and yield strength of 140 MPa while anode has a failure strain at 0.02 and a yield strength of 220 MPa. As the cathode and anode are closely placed and exposed to the similar mechanical loading, this incompatibility of cathode and anode in strength and stiffness could be the reason of battery internal failure.

claims.claim: The aluminum foil current collector under dynamic loading is so brittle that it nearly fails at the elastic stage, and as strain rate increases, yield stress increases but failure strain decreases for electrodes and current collectors.
claims.quote: The aluminum foil under dynamic loading is so brittle that it nearly fails at the elastic stage. In this study, both cathode and anode are treated and calibrated as transverse isotropic elastic-plastic materials with strain-rate effects... as the strain rate increases, the yield stress increases but the failure strain of metal foil decreases.

claims.claim: The separator exhibits a strain rate effect where stress level increases by 100% from quasi-static to dynamic condition, and failure strain decreases to 0.4 at a strain rate of 2.5 s⁻¹.
claims.quote: The strain rate effect of this separator is remarkable: the stress level increases by 100% from quasi-static condition to dynamic condition and the failure strain decreases to 0.4 at a strain-rate of 2.5 s⁻¹.

claims.claim: The anode specimen collapses at 56 MPa under compression and cracks propagate through the thickness direction, and the integrated battery sample compression failure is triggered by the fracture onset of the anode.
claims.quote: Anode specimen collapses at 56 MPa under compression and cracks go through the thickness direction, making the specimen split into pieces... It can be observed that the integrated battery sample has the stress-strain curve shape and the failure strain quite similar to the anode. This implies that the compression failure of the whole battery cell structure is triggered by the fracture onset of anode.

claims.claim: The normal failure strength of the cathode interface is 2 MPa, the anode interface is 0.3 MPa, and the separator interface is approximately 0.01 MPa.
claims.quote: The test results show that the normal failure strength of the cathode interface is 2 MPa, while that of anode interface is 0.3 MPa. The separator interface is much weaker, just about 0.01 MPa, but existence of this interface strength is essential for keeping the integrity of the battery cell.

claims.claim: Under dynamic impact, voltage drops before the first peak force, implying that internal short circuit may happen earlier under impact than under quasi-static conditions where voltage drops around the force peak.
claims.quote: the voltage drops around the force peak under the quasi-static condition, however, voltage under dynamic impact drops before the first peak force, implying that the internal short circuit may happen earlier under the impact.

claims.claim: Batteries at 30% and 60% SOC became smoky with temperatures above 100°C after dynamic impact, while batteries at 0% and 100% SOC only reached approximately 50°C and passed the impact tests.
claims.quote: the batteries of 30%, 60% SOCs have been smoky and temperature of which increase above 100 °C, revealing the ISC occurred inside. The batteries of 0%, 100% SOC only have a temperature shifting to 50 °C and pass the impact tests.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway is only observed under dynamic impact conditions and not under quasi-static conditions, with the rough fracture surface and crushed powdery residue identified as responsible for continuous internal short circuits and thermal runaway.
claims.quote: This can be verified through the comparison of quasi-static and dynamic tests that the thermal runaway is only observed under the dynamic condition... the rough fracture surface and the crushed powdery residue are responsible for the continuous internal short circuits and thermal runaway of the broken batteries.

claims.claim: Material failure is a necessary but insufficient condition for internal short circuit; if the fracture surface is smooth and debris does not connect different layers, ISC can stop without thermal runaway even after severe battery damage.
claims.quote: It is worth noting that material failure is a necessary and insufficient condition for ISC... if the fracture surface is smooth and debris does not connect different layers, the cathode and anode can still be separated by the remained separator. In this case, even if severe damage has happened to the battery, the ISC would stop without thermal runaway.

claims.claim: Strong restraint limiting the lateral movement of broken battery parts inside electronic devices or electric vehicles will maintain the ISC condition and increase thermal runaway probability.
claims.quote: Inside the electronic devices and the electrical vehicles, the batteries are embedded in a certain slot. This strong restraint, limiting the lateral movement of broken battery, will maintain the ISC condition and increase the thermal run-away probability.

claims.claim: The cathode has a three-stage compression stress-strain response with a turning point at 135 MPa, after which active particles are crushed to fill space between them and the force reaches a plateau at strain range from 0.18 to 0.32.
claims.quote: When the stress increases to 135 MPa, the curve encounters a turning point and enters the second stage. The active particles of cathode are being crushed to fill the space between them and the force reaches a plateau at the strain range from 0.18 to 0.32.
