claims.claim: An 18650 Li-ion cell with NCA cathode at 100% SOC produces 7.20 g of gaseous species within 1–10 s after reaching the onset temperature of thermal runaway (~160°C) during temperature-ramp tests in a chamber filled with inert gas.
claims.quote: an 18650 Li-ion cell with NCA cathodes at 100% SOC produces 7.20 g of gaseous species within 1–10 s after the cell reaches the onset temperature of thermal runaway (~160 C), during temperature-ramp tests in a chamber filled with inert gas.

claims.claim: The Reynolds number for vent flow from an 18650 cell during thermal runaway is estimated to be in the range of 3000–30000, indicating turbulent flow that decreases as gas discharge diminishes over time.
claims.quote: Revent = ρgugDvent/μg = ˙mg,vent/π Dventμg ∼ 3000 − 30000 ... Eq. (1) implies that the vent flow from an 18650 cell during thermal runaway is expected to be turbulent, while the degree of turbulence decreases as the gas discharge diminishes over time.

claims.claim: The pore Reynolds number inside an 18650 cell during thermal runaway is estimated at 300–3000, which exceeds the threshold of 100 above which Forchheimer's inertial term cannot be ignored.
claims.quote: Rep = ˙mg,vent/πnDpμg = 300 − 3000 which exceeds 100 [22,23] and hence Forchheimer's inertial term is considered in this study.

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, the total mass loss during thermal runaway was 20.5 g (45.1%), of which 7.20 g (15.9%) was due to gas generation, implying that 13.3 g of solid particles escaped with the gas burst flow.
claims.quote: the total mass loss in case of 100% SOC was 20.5 g (45.1%) while the mass loss due to gas generation was 7.20 g (15.9%) according to Golubkov et al. [5], which indicates that 13.3 g of solid particles broken into pieces during thermal runaway and escapes with the gas burst flow during thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The isentropic choked flow approximation predicts a ten times larger initial mass flow rate during venting compared to the Forchheimer-dominant flow model, which better matches the CFD model results.
claims.quote: whereas the isentropic chocked flow approximation used in the literature predicts a ten times larger mass flow rate during the initial stages of venting, our current model matches well with the Forchheimer-dominant flow model

claims.claim: The maximum internal gas pressure during thermal runaway increases with SOC: 4.25 bar at 100% SOC, 1.71 bar at 50% SOC, and 0.20 bar at 25% SOC.
claims.quote: The maximum internal gas pressures are 4.25 bar, 1.71 bar and 0.20 bar at 100%, 50% and 25% SOCs, respectively, indicating that the maximum gas pressure build-up during the thermal runaway increases with SOC.

claims.claim: The pressure required to split a 220 μm thick mild steel casing is less than 2 bar at temperatures above 600°C, making pressure-induced side-wall breach a high risk when internal pressure reaches 4.25 bar.
claims.quote: The pressure required to split a 220 μm thick mild steel casing was found to be < 2 bar at temperatures >600 C [3]. Thus, the pressure of 4.25 bar predicted to build up within the cells from our models, indicates that some cell designs are likely to undergo pressure-induced side-wall breach during thermal runaway

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, the contribution of gas-phase reactions to total heat generation is 3 times larger than the contribution from thermal abuse reactions occurring among cell components.
claims.quote: At 100% SOC, the contribution of the gas-phase reactions to the total heat generation is 3 times larger than that of the thermal abuse reactions taking place among the cell components.

claims.claim: At 25% SOC, carbon dioxide contributes 83.5% of the total mass of generated gas, whereas at 100% SOC, reactive gases (H2, CO, CH4) contribute 66.5% of the total generated gas mass.
claims.quote: carbon dioxide contributes 83.5% of the total mass of generated gas in case of 25% SOC. However, carbon dioxide only contributes 33.5% of the total mass of generated gas in case of 100% SOC, while reactive gases such as hydrogen, carbon monoxide and methane contribute to 66.5% of mass generated during thermal runaway at 100% SOC

claims.claim: The one-equation thermal abuse model used in this study has limitations in predicting when thermal runaway is mitigated during a rapid thermal ramp, and a multiple-equation model is required for such cases.
claims.quote: this one equation model has limitations in predicting when the thermal runaway is mitigated during a rapid thermal ramp. For such cases, multiple equation thermal abuse model is required to correctly model the thermal abuse behavior.

claims.claim: Gas released during the first venting stage (cell wall breach) represents only 0.61%, 1.22%, and 2.83% of total gas generated at 100%, 50%, and 25% SOC respectively, indicating cell wall breach is a secondary mechanism for gas release.
claims.quote: 0.044 g, 0.054 g and 0.063 g of gases are released due to cell wall breach at 100%, 50% and 25% SOCs, respectively. These also correspond to 0.61%, 1.22% and 2.83% of the total mass of gas generated through the whole processes, respectively, indicating that the cell wall breach is the secondary mechanism for the gas release during thermal abuse conditions.

claims.claim: The simulated flame length at 6 seconds from onset of thermal runaway at 100% SOC is 39.2 cm, which agrees with the experimentally measured maximum flame length of approximately 35 cm for a 100% SOC cell reported by Chen et al.
claims.quote: the flame length is 39.2 cm at 6sec from the onset of thermal runaway ... Chen et al. [72] experimentally shows that the maximum flame length is around 35 cm (39.2 cm in this study) for the 100% SOC

claims.claim: At around 133°C, the impedance increased by about two orders of magnitude for PLBs and four orders of magnitude for cell C (LIB), corresponding to the polyethylene separator shutdown mechanism.
claims.quote: At around 133 8C, the impedance increased by about two orders for the PLBs, and four orders of magnitude for cell C. Polyethylene separators have shutdown temperatures between 130 and 140 8C, which corresponds to the melting point of the PE separator.

claims.claim: Above 140°C, the OCV dropped to zero indicating an internal short-circuit due to separator meltdown, suggesting that pinholes created in the separator at meltdown are large enough to create an internal short-circuit.
claims.quote: Above 140 8C, the OCV dropped to zero indicating an internal short-circuit due the separator meltdown suggesting that the pinholes created in the separator at meltdown are large enough to create an internal short-circuit.

claims.claim: An impedance increase by only two orders of magnitude at the shutdown temperature may not be sufficient for complete cell shutdown, meaning cells may continue to overcharge at lower rates and remain a safety hazard.
claims.quote: An increase in the impedance by two orders of magnitude may not be large enough for complete shutdown, i.e. cells may continue to overcharge although at lower rates and this will continue to be a safety hazard.

claims.claim: The onset-of-thermal-runaway temperature (OTRT) decreased by at least 10°C when cells A and C were charged from 25% to 75% SOC, while cell B showed SOC-independent OTRT.
claims.quote: The OTRT was found to depend on the state of charge for cells A and C, i.e. it decreased by at least 10 8C when the cells were charged from 25 to 75% SOC. On the other hand, the OTRT was SOC-independent for cell B.

claims.claim: Cell C (LIB using LiBF4/EC–GBL electrolyte) showed no rupture, no swelling, and no trace of fire after thermal runaway, attributed to the thermal and electrochemical stability of the LiBF4/EC–GBL electrolyte against oxidation conditions of the LiCoO2 cathode.
claims.quote: This study also revealed no rupture, no swelling, and no trace of fire for the LIB. This is attributable to the thermal and electrochemical stability of LiBF4/EC–GBL electrolyte against oxidation condition of the LiCoO2 cathode material.

claims.claim: Under thermal abuse at 75 W, electrical failure times were approximately 443 s for LFP cells and 185 s for NMC cells.
claims.quote: Electrical failure times for the 75 W thermal tests are measured at approximately 443 s and 185 s for the LFP and NMC cells respectively.

claims.claim: In thermal-overcharge combination tests, a reconnection phenomenon occurs where the battery begins accepting charge again after going open loop, attributed to melting of the separator causing an internal short circuit.
claims.quote: The reconnection refers to when the battery begins accepting charge again after going open loop which is seen on the current versus time plots in Fig. 8(b) and (d). Potentially, this could be attributed to melting of the separator within the cell providing an internal short circuit condition.

claims.claim: Thermal-overcharge combination tests produced the most violent failures of both LFP and NMC battery types, more energetic than either thermal or overcharge tests alone.
claims.quote: Thermal-overcharge combination tests showed the most violent failures of both battery types. These significant energetic failures are correlated with a reconnection phenomena.

claims.claim: Pressure waves observed in axial schlieren images 0.1 ms after LFP cell failure are approximately sonic (approximately Mach 1 shock waves), implying high enough internal pressures to produce a sonic condition at the vent plane.
claims.quote: This density change is not quantified, but its presence implies that these pressure waves are approximately sonic [19]. As the series waves does not coalesce, the resulting temperature difference and therefore sound speed increase across each wave is minimal. This observation implies that the shock waves are very weak, approximately Mach 1 shock waves.

claims.claim: Schlieren imaging cannot provide quantitative density measurements in multi-species gas environments because density variations cannot be deconvoluted from species gradients.
claims.quote: Schlieren imaging visualizes refractive index gradients, which in a single gas system can be directly related to gas density, but in the presence of multiple gas species the density variations cannot be deconvoluted from the species gradients.

claims.claim: In the NMC combination test, a secondary failure coinciding with reconnection at 234.9 s was the only combustion event observed during the comparative test series, and combustion momentarily spread across the entirety of the test chamber internal volume.
claims.quote: The NMC experienced a secondary failure at the moment of reconnection even though the battery was already venting. This secondary failure of the NMC battery displayed the only combustion event observed during the comparative test series. Combustion during this test momentarily spread across the entirety of the test chamber internal volume.

claims.claim: Under overcharge conditions in lithium metal oxide cathodes, cathode decomposition results in generation of oxygen gas and lithium ions, and intercalated lithium within the electrolyte can react to form hydrocarbons, presenting a significant flammability risk.
claims.quote: Under overcharge conditions, cathode decomposition results in generation of oxygen gas and lithium ions. Additionally, intercalated lithium within the electrolyte can react resulting in formation of hydrocarbons. The presence of both oxygen and hydrocarbons presents a significant flammability risk.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway (TR) in lithium-ion batteries can be prevented by applying liquid nitrogen (LN) before the surface temperature reaches the critical TR suppression temperature (Ttr,cs), with threshold values ranging from 270.2 ± 0.7°C for 25% SOC to 172.2 ± 1.1°C for 100% SOC.
claims.quote: TR can be prevented by applying LN before the critical TR suppression temperature is reached (Ttr,cs). The threshold values range from 270.2 ± 0.7 °C to 170.2 ± 1.1 °C for SOC of 25%–100%.

claims.claim: When the surface temperature (Tw) of the battery exceeds the critical TR suppression temperature (Ttr,cs), thermal runaway is unavoidable even during continuous application of liquid nitrogen.
claims.quote: When the surface temperature of the battery (Tw) exceeds Ttr,cs, TR is unavoidable even during the application of LN.

claims.claim: The delaying effect of liquid nitrogen on thermal runaway onset is weakened as the battery surface temperature (Tw) approaches the triggering temperature (Ttr), and for 100% SOC batteries, LN loses its delaying effect when Tw exceeds 176°C (only 15.1°C below Ttr).
claims.quote: when the Tw of the 100%SOC is 176°C, which is 15.1°C lower than the triggering temperature, but the delayed time at this time is only 1s. In that case, once the Tw＞176°C, LN will lose the delaying effect on the TR of 100%SOC.

claims.claim: Only 29.3 g of liquid nitrogen sprayed on a 9.24 Wh (18650, 100% SOC) battery reduces the surface temperature from approximately 700°C to less than 100°C within 80 seconds.
claims.quote: only 29.3 g of LN can reduce the temperature of 9.24 Wh (2.2 mAh × 4.2 V) battery from about 700°C to no more than 100°C within 80s, indicating that a very small of LN can control the triggering battery to a relatively safe level.

claims.claim: The effective heat utilization efficiency (εe) of liquid nitrogen decreases from 80% to 37.8% as the mass of LN sprayed (mLN) increases from 5.86 g to 29.3 g, indicating that cooling efficiency degrades with increasing LN application.
claims.quote: The value of εe decreases from 80% to 37.8% when mLN rises from 5.86 to 29.3 g, implying that the cooling efficiency of LN gradually decreases with increasing mLN.

claims.claim: The calculated average cooling rate (cave) of liquid nitrogen decreases from 10.3°C/s to 2.84°C/s as the battery surface temperature decreases from 800°C to 0°C, indicating that the cooling effect of LN weakens with decreasing surface temperature.
claims.quote: the cave decreases from 10.3°C/s to 2.84°C/s when the surface temperature reduces from 800°C to 0°C, indicating that the cooling effect of LN is weakened with decreasing Tw.

claims.claim: Compared with water mist, CO2, and HFC-227ea fire extinguishing agents, liquid nitrogen achieves the greatest temperature reduction (450.6°C in 60 s) and highest average cooling rate (7.51°C/s) at the lowest working pressure (0.04 MPa).
claims.quote: compared with several other fire extinguishing agents, LN owns the best cooling performance under the lowest working pressure... Liquid nitrogen: 60 s, 0.04 MPa, 450.6°C reduction, 7.51°C/s average cooling rate.

claims.claim: Application of 80 seconds of liquid nitrogen to a brand-new 18650 lithium-ion battery did not cause significant degradation in cycle performance.
claims.quote: the battery with 80 s LN application showed no obvious performance degradation, demonstrating that the application of 80 s LN did not show a significant impact on the cycle performance of the battery.

claims.claim: With increasing SOC, lithium-ion batteries exhibit lower thermal runaway triggering temperature (Ttr), shorter onset time (ttr), and higher maximum temperature (Tmax), with 100% SOC batteries showing Ttr of 191.1 ± 0.5°C and Tmax of 732.9 ± 16.4°C versus 278.9 ± 0.8°C and 608.1 ± 45.6°C for 25% SOC.
claims.quote: with the increasing of SOC, the battery generally exhibits lower Ttr, shorter ttr, and higher Tmax... 25%: Ttr = 278.9 ± 0.8°C, Tmax = 608.1 ± 45.6°C; 100%: Ttr = 191.1 ± 0.5°C, Tmax = 732.9 ± 16.4°C.

claims.claim: The internal cooling rate of a 100% SOC 18650 battery during 80 s liquid nitrogen application increases with application time, reaching a peak of 10.3°C/s and an average of 5.7°C/s, indicating that the cooling effect on the battery interior becomes more effective as application time increases.
claims.quote: the internal cooling rate increases with the application time of LN and owns a peak of 10.3°C/s and an average of 5.7°C/s, suggesting that the cooling effect of LN to the inside the battery becomes more superior as the application time increases.

claims.claim: Oven tests at 190°C were able to capture thermal runaway characteristics in batteries with 100% SOC only; batteries at 50% and 0% SOC did not undergo thermal runaway during the 6-hour oven heating programme at 190°C.
claims.quote: Oven test at 190°C was able to capture thermal runaway characteristics in batteries with 100% SOC batteries only. The 6 hours heating process during oven test at a preset temperature of 190°C was insufficient to induce the heat-activated exothermic reactions in batteries at SOC of 0% and 50%.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway of 100% SOC batteries in oven tests resulted in a maximum temperature of 738–783°C, whereas ARC tests produced maximum temperatures between 404 and 522°C, because some battery energy content was released slowly during the long ARC self-heating period, leaving less energy for rapid release during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The recorded maximum temperatures from ARC test were between 404 and 522°C, lower than oven tests since some of the battery energy content was released slowly during the long self-heating period to increase the battery temperature to beyond point of no return.

claims.claim: In oven tests, the presence of two unconnected full-charged batteries reduced thermal runaway induction time by 8.6% to 115.98 min compared to 126.92 min for a single cell.
claims.quote: A single battery recorded a thermal runaway induction time of 126.92 min. The time is reduced by 8.6% to 115.98 min as two batteries were present in the oven.

claims.claim: In oven tests, two full-charged batteries with parallel electrical connection (doubling total capacity to 5.0 Ah) further reduced thermal runaway induction time by 15% to 107.72 min compared to a single cell.
claims.quote: The presence of parallel electrical connection resulted in a further inclined towards thermal runaway by 15% to 107.72 min.

claims.claim: ARC operating in Heat-Wait-Seek mode induced thermal runaway in a 50% SOC battery and caused exothermic thermal decomposition (self-heating) but not thermal runaway in a 0% SOC battery, due to the longer uninterrupted heating process permitted by the ARC's integrated safety features.
claims.quote: ARC was able to induce thermal runaway in 50% SOC battery due to its longer heating process. The prolonged experimental period was able to induce the heat-activated exothermic reactions in battery with 0% SOC and resulted in self-heating behaviour but was not sufficient to cause thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The presence of aluminium current collector ash and unburnt copper current collector in oven test residues indicated that the internal battery temperature exceeded 659°C (aluminium melting point) but remained below 1083°C (copper melting point).
claims.quote: The sight of aluminium current collector ash and the presence of unburnt copper current collector in the residues indicated that the battery internal temperature reached a point higher than 659°C, which is the aluminium melting temperature but lower than 1083°C, which is the copper melting point.

claims.claim: The SEI layer undergoes exothermic decomposition at temperatures between 90°C and 120°C, and this is identified as the first exothermic reaction that occurs during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The SEI layer is unstable at high temperature. Physical penetration, overheating and overcharge compel the SEI to decompose. The reaction is exothermic and takes place at temperature between 90°C and 120°C.

claims.claim: In ARC tests, reducing battery SOC from 100% to 50% increased thermal runaway induction time by 22.5% from 2046.89 min to 2507.34 min, while reducing SOC to 0% resulted in no thermal runaway being observed.
claims.quote: A single full-charged battery recorded a thermal runaway induction time of 2046.89 min. The time is reduced by 14.4%–1752.0 min as two batteries were present in the ARC chamber. Meanwhile, reducing the available battery capacity to half resulted in an increment of induction time by 22.5%–2507.34 min. As the battery capacity is further reduced to zero, no thermal runaway was observed.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway (TR) is not triggered during 0.4 C and 0.5 C overcharge of NCM811 pouch cells, with surface peak temperatures of 94.14°C and 86.61°C respectively, because the low charging current results in slower ohmic heating and the self-generated heat rate is offset by the external environment.
claims.quote: TR is not caused during the 0.4 C and 0.5 C overcharge process. This is attributed to the low charging current, which results in slower ohmic heating... The self-generated heat rate is offset by the external environment, which prevents heat accumulation. Therefore, without sufficient heat being generated to initiate the subsequent reaction, the chain reaction is halted and TR is avoided.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway is triggered in NCM811 pouch cells at overcharge rates of 0.8 C, 1 C, 1.5 C, 2 C, and 3 C, with TR trigger temperatures of 88.26°C, 93.71°C, 91.63°C, 93.82°C, and 89.56°C respectively, per the IWG GTR EVS criterion of temperature rise exceeding 1°C/s.
claims.quote: the LIBs suffer TR under overcharge conditions at 0.8 C, 1 C, 1.5 C, 2 C, and 3 C... According to the IWG GTR EVS [10]... the TR is triggered when the temperature rise exceeds 1°C/s, where the surface peak temperatures are marked. Corresponding to 0.8 C, 1 C, 1.5 C, 2 C, and 3 C overcharge, the TR trigger temperatures are 88.26°C, 93.71°C, 91.63°C, 93.82°C, and 89.56°C, respectively.

claims.claim: Exothermic side reactions contribute 63.48%–68.97% of total accumulated heat across overcharge rates of 0.8 C to 3 C in NCM811 pouch cells, making them the primary contribution to thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The respective proportions of Qside are 68.97 %, 65.51 %, 63.6 %, 63.7 %, and 63.48 %, respectively. This indicates that exothermic side reactions are the primary contributions to TR.

claims.claim: In NCM811 overcharge-induced thermal runaway, a voltage drop following a sharp voltage rise indicates that Li dendrites on the anode have bridged the cathode and anode, causing an internal short circuit.
claims.quote: A significant rise in voltage, followed by an extreme drop, indicates that Li dendrites on the anode have bridged the cathode and anode. This creates a strong support for overcharge-induced TR.

claims.claim: During overcharge of NCM811 cells, oxygen release from the cathode begins at approximately 4.9 V (m/z=32 signal onset), and CO2, H2, and C2H4 gases evolve at approximately 5.09 V, 5.33 V, and 5.59 V respectively, as measured by on-line electrochemical mass spectrometry.
claims.quote: The m/z = 32 (O2) signal begins to rise at about 4.9 V... The m/z = 44 (CO2), m/z = 2 (H2), and m/z = 28 (C2H4) are evolved at approximately 5.09 V, 5.33 V, and 5.59 V, respectively.

claims.claim: The onset temperature of thermal decomposition for the 100% SOC NCM811 cathode is 211.56°C with ΔH of −51.56 J/g, whereas the deeply delithiated 140% SOC cathode decomposes at a lower onset temperature of 199.8°C with higher heat release of ΔH = −64.72 J/g.
claims.quote: the onset temperature of thermal decomposition for the 100 % SOC cathode is 211.56°C... the 100 % SOC cathode displays three exothermic peaks... with ΔH of −51.56 J/g. Compared with 100 % SOC, the deeply delithiated (140 % SOC) cathode decomposes at 199.8°C and exhibits two exothermic peaks... with ΔH of −64.72 J/g.

claims.claim: The 140% SOC NCM811 cathode begins to release oxygen at 202.54°C, which is lower than the 100% SOC cathode onset of 215.48°C, and the 140% SOC cathode exhibits a stronger ion current, indicating that deeper delithiation reduces the oxygen release onset temperature.
claims.quote: the 100 % and 140 % SOC cathodes begin to release oxygen at 215.48°C and 202.54°C, respectively, while the 140 % SOC cathode exhibits a stronger ion current.

claims.claim: The thermal decomposition onset temperature of the combined cathode + anode (Ca+An) mixture decreases from 198.47°C at 100% SOC to 175.9°C at 140% SOC, while heat release increases from −616.308 J/g to −693.3 J/g, indicating that overcharge worsens the redox crosstalk reaction severity.
claims.quote: At 100 % and 140 % SOC, the initial thermal decomposition temperatures of Ca+An are 198.47°C and 175.9°C, respectively. They exhibit a broad peak at 240.52°C and 248.63°C with ΔH=−616.308 J/g and ΔH=−693.3 J/g, respectively.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway trigger temperature for the 140% SOC pouch cell (187.26°C) is lower than for the 100% SOC cell (201.82°C), and the peak temperature is higher (745.18°C vs. 723.69°C), attributable to premature oxygen release from the cathode and Li deposition reactions at the anode.
claims.quote: the TR trigger temperatures for 100 % and 140 % SOC are 201.82°C and 187.26°C, with the peak temperatures at 723.69°C and 745.18°C, respectively. At elevated charge states, the TR trigger temperature is reduced, and the peak temperature is elevated. This can be attributed to the premature release of oxygen from the cathode and the Li deposition reaction at the anode.

claims.claim: The lattice volume of the NCM811 cathode contracts by 3.67% at 140% SOC compared to the fresh state, and the lattice parameter c/a decreases, indicating collapse of the layered structure due to excessive delithiation.
claims.quote: Lattice V is also contracted by 3.67 % at 140 % SOC. Lattice parameter a remains stable, while lattice c/a is decreased. This suggests that the TM interlayer distance of cathode is reduced, causing the collapse of the layered structure.

claims.claim: The I(003)/I(104) ratio in NCM811 cathode XRD falls below 1.2 at 130% SOC, indicating a critical threshold at which cation mixing (Li/Ni disorder) becomes significant during overcharge.
claims.quote: I(003)/I(104) is considered to evaluate cation mixing... and I(003)/I(104) is reduced below 1.2 at 130 % SOC.

claims.claim: The Li content of the NCM811 anode at 140% SOC is elevated by 38.48% compared to 100% SOC as measured by ICP-OES, confirming Li plating during overcharge.
claims.quote: Compared with 100 % SOC, the Li content of 140 % SOC is elevated by 38.48 %, further confirming the occurrence of Li plating.

claims.claim: The anode Raman D/G band intensity ratio (ID/IG) increases from 0.27 to 0.57 with increasing overcharge capacity, indicating progressive structural disorder and degradation of the graphite anode.
claims.quote: With increasing overcharge capacity, the Raman peaks of the failed anode are significantly broadened, accompanied by an elevation of the ID/IG ratio from 0.27 to 0.57.

claims.claim: The onset temperature of thermal decomposition for the combined anode + electrolyte (An+Ele) system decreases from 158.59°C at 100% SOC to 140.75°C at 140% SOC, and total heat release increases from −318.69 J/g to −346.31 J/g, attributed to more extensive lithiated anode at higher SOC intensifying interaction with the electrolyte.
claims.quote: This presents a broad peak commencing at 158.59°C... for 100 % SOC. The broad peak is primarily attributed to the chemical crosstalk reaction between the exposed LiC6 and the electrolyte. In contrast, the onset temperature of thermal decomposition is 140.75°C... for 140 % SOC. The total heat (−346.31 J/g) for 140 % SOC is higher than the sum of heat (−318.69 J/g) for 100 % SOC.

claims.claim: The NCM811 cathode crystal structure transforms from the rhombohedral (R) phase to the spinel (S) phase upon overcharge to 140% SOC, as confirmed by HRTEM, FFT, and SAED analysis showing a mixture of R and S phases with alternating O1 and O3 stacking resulting in a severely disordered lattice.
claims.quote: We observe that the cathode crystal structure is transformed from R phase to the spinel (S) phase upon overcharge. The alternating stacking of O1 and O3 results in a severely disordered lattice, with noticeable differences in lattice orientation across different regions.

claims.claim: The total LIB resistance during overcharge peaks at 3.488 Ω at a voltage of 24.18 V just before dropping to 0 V, with polarization resistance contributing 84.77% of the total resistance.
claims.quote: As LIB voltage is elevated to 24.18 V and then rapidly decreases to 0 V, the LIB total resistance is rapidly increased, peaking at 3.488 Ω... Among these, the contribution of polarization resistance is calculated to be 84.77 %.

claims.claim: For the combined cathode + anode + electrolyte system at 140% SOC, the high-temperature exothermic peak occurs at 254.96°C with ΔH = −819.13 J/g, compared to −742.84 J/g for the 100% SOC system, with the increase attributed to more oxygen from the cathode and more Li from the anode surface inducing additional crosstalk reactions with the electrolyte.
claims.quote: for 140 % SOC state, while the low-temperature exothermic peak appears at 158.02°C, the high-temperature exothermic peak occurs at 254.96°C with ΔH = −819.13 J/g. This is attributed to more oxygen from the cathode and Li from the anode surface, which induce crosstalk reactions with the electrolyte, promoting more heat generation.

claims.claim: The F and P contents on the graphite anode surface (by EDS) increase progressively from 0.7 wt% F and 0.08 wt% P at 100% SOC to 16.53 wt% F and 1.69 wt% P at 140% SOC, indicating that LiPF6 decomposition is progressively enhanced with increasing overcharge.
claims.quote: The F (0.7–16.53 wt. %) and P (0.08–1.69 wt. %) contents are elevated with increasing overcharge capacity, revealing that the decomposition of LiPF6 is progressively enhanced.

claims.claim: The cathode O content decreases at 130% SOC in EDS analysis, preliminarily confirming oxygen release from the highly delithiated cathode during overcharge.
claims.quote: The O content decreases at 130 % SOC, which preliminarily confirms the release of oxygen from the highly delithiated cathode.

claims.claim: The gas volume generated inside NCM811 pouch cells during overcharge increases from 6.17 mL at 125% SOC to 18.18 mL at 140% SOC for CO2, and from 5.10 mL to 11.22 mL for CO, partly attributed to continuous electrolyte decomposition under elevated voltage.
claims.quote: the gas volume is increased from 6.17 mL to 18.18 mL, which is partly attributed to the continuous decomposition of the electrolyte under elevated voltage... the gas volume is increased from 5.10 mL to 11.22 mL, originating from the high-voltage oxidative decomposition of EC and the crosstalk reactions between DMC and Li+.

claims.claim: The NCM811 cathode structure undergoes a phase transformation sequence from O3 through H1–H3 mixed phase to O1 with increasing overcharge, exacerbating thermodynamic instability.
claims.quote: The changes in lattice parameters suggest that the cathode structure is gradually transformed from O3 to H1–H3 and eventually to O1, thereby exacerbating the thermodynamic instability.

claims.claim: The Ca+Ele (140% SOC) system exhibits a lower thermal decomposition onset temperature (192.65°C) and higher heat release (−256.57 J/g) compared to Ca+Ele (100% SOC) at 201.48°C onset and −221.7 J/g, attributed to higher oxygen release from the cathode at elevated voltages promoting earlier decomposition.
claims.quote: at 140 % SOC, the thermal decomposition temperature of Ca+Ele occurs at a lower temperature (192.65°C), and the heat release (−256.57 J/g) increases compared to 100 % SOC. This is attributed to the higher oxygen release from the cathode at elevated voltages, thus promoting earlier thermal decomposition and greater heat release.

claims.claim: Under overcharge conditions, the phase transition from LiC6 to LiC12 in the graphite anode predominantly takes place within the SOC range of 125–140%, at which stage the anode structure undergoes an irreversible transformation.
claims.quote: The phase transition from LiC6 to LiC12 predominantly takes place within the SOC range of 125–140 %. At this stage, the anode structure is subjected to an irreversible transformation.

claims.claim: LiFePO4 features strong P–O covalent bonds and negligible lattice oxygen release, resulting in superior thermal stability and less intense exothermic reactions compared to NCM811, which exhibits significant lattice oxygen release and exothermic oxygen–electrolyte reactions during overcharge.
claims.quote: LiFePO4, in contrast, features strong P–O covalent bonds and negligible lattice oxygen release, resulting in superior thermal stability and less intense exothermic reactions.

claims.claim: Once the LIB internal temperature reaches the thermal decomposition threshold of the SEI during overcharge, a self-sustaining reaction regime is triggered, heightening the risk of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Once the LIB temperature is reached the thermal decomposition threshold of the SEI, the self-sustaining reaction regime is triggered, thereby heightening the risk of TR.
